6 Description GPT5: Systematic study of knowledge and justified belief, including sources (perception, memory, testimony, a priori), standards of justification, skepticism, and the aims and value of cognitive life.
6.1 Description Gemini: The branch of philosophy concerned with the nature, origin, and limits of knowledge. It explores what knowledge is, how we acquire it, what its sources are, and how to distinguish it from mere belief.
6.2 Description SEP: The term "epistemology" comes from the Greek words "episteme" and "logos". "Episteme" can be translated as "knowledge" or "understanding" or "acquaintance", while "logos" can be translated as "account" or "argument" or "reason". Just as each of these different translations captures some facet of the meaning of these Greek terms, so too does each translation capture a different facet of epistemology itself. Although the term "epistemology" is no more than a couple of centuries old, the field of epistemology is at least as old as any in philosophy. In different parts of its extensive history, different facets of epistemology have attracted attention. Plato's epistemology was an attempt to understand what it was to know, and how knowledge (unlike mere true opinion) is good for the knower. Locke's epistemology was an attempt to understand the operations of human understanding, Kant's epistemology was an attempt to understand the conditions of the possibility of human understanding, and Russell's epistemology was an attempt to understand how modern science could be justified by appeal to sensory experience. Much recent work in formal epistemology is an attempt to understand how our degrees of confidence are rationally constrained by our evidence, and much recent work in feminist epistemology is an attempt to understand the ways in which interests affect our evidence, and affect our rational constraints more generally. In all these cases, epistemology seeks to understand one or another kind of cognitive success (or, correspondingly, cognitive failure). This entry surveys the varieties of cognitive success, and some recent efforts to understand some of those varieties.
6.3 Description SEP: The study of knowledge and justified belief. Core questions concern knowledge's nature (justified true belief plus conditions to avoid Gettier cases), justification's structure (foundationalism, coherentism, infinitism), skepticism, and sources of knowledge (perception, reason, testimony). Contemporary epistemology addresses social epistemology and formal methods.

6 Description GPT5: Taxonomy of routes to belief formation that can yield justification or knowledge, such as perception, memory, introspection, reasoning, intuition, testimony, and a priori methods.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of the origins and pathways through which knowledge is acquired. This category includes sources like perception, memory, reason, and testimony from others.

6 Description GPT5: Knowledge or justification independent of particular experiences; often linked to necessity, analyticity, or rational insight, and contrasted with a posteriori knowledge derived from empirical observation.
6.1 Description Gemini: Knowledge or justification that is independent of experience. It is based on reason and logical deduction rather than empirical evidence (e.g., "All bachelors are unmarried").

6 Description GPT5: Explores whether truths knowable a priori are necessarily true and whether necessity entails apriority, distinguishing modal status from epistemic route and examining counterexamples (e.g., necessary a posteriori).
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical investigation into the relationship between a priori knowledge and necessary truths-propositions that could not possibly be false.

6 Description GPT5: Accounts of how mathematical truths can be known without empirical confirmation, through proof, abstraction, or conceptual insight, and debates over whether mathematical knowledge is revisable or fallible.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of how mathematical truths are known a priori, through reason alone, without direct reliance on sensory experience.

6 Description GPT5: Class of judgments that extend knowledge yet are knowable a priori; traditionally associated with geometry and arithmetic and reassessed in light of logicism, formalism, and empiricism.
6.1 Description Gemini: A concept, most famously discussed by Immanuel Kant, referring to knowledge that is known a priori but is not analytically true (i.e., not true simply by definition). It is both informative about the world and knowable through reason.

6 Description GPT5: Competing explanations of a priori justification-rational intuition, conceptual analysis, conventionalism, reliabilist metasemantics, and pragmatic or heuristic views of a priori methods.
6.1 Description Gemini: The various philosophical explanations for how a priori knowledge is possible, ranging from rationalism (innate ideas) to linguistic theories (analytic truths).

6 Description GPT5: A seeming or intellectual presentation that makes a proposition appear true without inferential support; investigated for its phenomenology, reliability, and evidential force in philosophy and mathematics.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ability to acquire knowledge or justification without conscious reasoning. In epistemology, it is often treated as a direct, non-inferential source of belief or evidence.
6.2 Description SEP: This entry addresses the nature and epistemological role of intuition by considering the following questions: (1) What are intuitions?, (2) What roles do they serve in philosophical (and other "armchair") inquiry?, (3) Ought they serve such roles?, (4) What are the implications of the empirical investigation of intuitions for their proper roles?, and (in the supplementary document titled "The Logical Structure of the Method of Cases") (5) What is the content of intuitions prompted by the consideration of hypothetical cases?
6.3 Description SEP: Non-inferential seeming or intellectual appearance. Philosophical intuitions about cases (thought experiments) guide theory construction. Questions concern intuitions' nature (beliefs, dispositions, or sui generis), evidential status, and reliability. Experimental philosophy challenges reliance on intuitions by showing demographic variation.

6 Description GPT5: Evaluates whether intuitions provide prima facie justification, how they interact with perception and inference, and when debunking explanations (e.g., bias, heuristics) undercut their evidential value.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of how intuition functions as a source of knowledge or justified belief, and under what conditions intuitions can be considered reliable evidence.

6 Description GPT5: Metaethical view that some moral truths are self-evident and knowable without the need for inference or empirical investigation. Paired with pluralism about basic duties or properties and defended against skepticism and disagreement.
6.1 Description Gemini: A meta-ethical theory suggesting that fundamental moral truths are known directly through intuition, without the need for inference or empirical investigation.

6 Description GPT5: Analyses of what intuitions are-appearances, judgments, dispositions, or seemings-how they relate to concepts, and whether their content is conceptual, perceptual-like, or modal.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical inquiry into what intuitions are, their cognitive origins, and their characteristics as mental states, distinct from perception, belief, and reasoning.

6 Description GPT5: Cognitive process of forming conclusions, judgments, or inferences from facts or premises. Includes deductive, inductive, and abductive forms and psychological mechanisms that implement them.
6.1 Description Gemini: The cognitive process of forming conclusions, judgments, or inferences from facts or premises.

6 Description GPT5: "Inference to the best explanation". Selecting hypotheses that would make observed data most intelligible. (A form of logical inference that starts with an observation or set of observations and then seeks the simplest and most likely explanation or hypothesis.). Evaluated by explanatory virtues such as simplicity, coherence, and unifying power.
6.1 Description Gemini: A form of logical inference that starts with an observation or set of observations and then seeks the simplest and most likely explanation or hypothesis. It is often called "inference to the best explanation."

6 Description GPT5: A connected set of premises offered in support of a conclusion; assessed for validity or strength, soundness, relevance, and dialectical appropriateness.
6.1 Description Gemini: A set of reasons or premises offered in support of a conclusion, intended to persuade others that the conclusion is true.
6.3 Description SEP: Arguments are central to philosophy and pervasive in science, law, education, and politics. An argument is typically a set of propositions where some (premises) provide support for another (conclusion). The study of argumentation is interdisciplinary, examining logical structure, rhetorical effectiveness, and the norms governing rational persuasion.

6 Description GPT5: The disciplined practice of analyzing and appraising arguments and evidence, clarifying concepts, identifying assumptions, and detecting fallacies to improve belief-formation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The objective analysis and evaluation of an issue in order to form a judgment. It involves interpreting, analyzing, and evaluating information to construct a well-supported argument.

6 Description GPT5: Inference where, if the premises are true, the conclusion must be true; central notions include validity, proof, and logical consequence.
6.1 Description Gemini: A form of reasoning in which a conclusion follows logically and necessarily from a set of premises. If the premises are true, the conclusion must also be true.

6 Description GPT5: Patterns of reasoning that are defective or unreliable in ways that undermine an argument’s rational support for its conclusion, even if the conclusion happens to be true.
6.1 Description Gemini: Errors in reasoning that undermine the logic of an argument. They can be formal (related to the structure of the argument) or informal (related to the content).
6.2 Description SEP: Two competing conceptions of fallacies are that they are false but popular beliefs and that they are deceptively bad arguments. These we may distinguish as the belief and argument conceptions of fallacies. Academic writers who have given the most attention to the subject of fallacies insist on, or at least prefer, the argument conception of fallacies, but the belief conception is prevalent in popular and non-scholarly discourse. As we shall see, there are yet other conceptions of what fallacies are, but the present inquiry focuses on the argument conception of fallacies. Being able to detect and avoid fallacies has been viewed as a supplement to criteria of good reasoning. The knowledge of fallacies is needed to arm us against the most enticing missteps we might take with arguments-so thought not only Aristotle but also the early nineteenth century logicians Richard Whately and John Stuart Mill. But as the course of logical theory from the late nineteenth-century forward turned more and more to axiomatic systems and formal languages, the study of reasoning and natural language argumentation received much less attention, and hence developments in the study of fallacies almost came to a standstill. Until well past the middle of the twentieth century, discussions of fallacies were for the most part relegated to introductory level textbooks. It was only when philosophers realized the ill fit between formal logic, on the one hand, and natural language reasoning and argumentation, on the other, that the interest in fallacies has returned. Since the 1970s the utility of knowing about fallacies has been acknowledged (Johnson and Blair 1993), and the way in which fallacies are incorporated into theories of argumentation has been taken as a sign of a theory's level of adequacy (Biro and Siegel 2007, van Eemeren 2010). In modern fallacy studies it is common to distinguish formal and informal fallacies. Formal fallacies are those readily seen to be instances of identifiable invalid logical forms such as undistributed middle and denying the antecedent. Although many of the informal fallacies are also invalid arguments, it is generally thought to be more profitable, from the points of view of both recognition and understanding, to bring their weaknesses to light through analyses that do not involve appeal to formal languages. For this reason it has become the practice to eschew the symbolic language of formal logic in the analysis of these fallacies; hence the term 'informal fallacy' has gained wide currency. In the following essay, which is in four parts, it is what is considered the informal-fallacy literature that will be reviewed. Part 1 is an introduction to the core fallacies as brought to us by the tradition of the textbooks. Part 2 reviews the history of the development of the conceptions of fallacies as it is found from Aristotle to Copi. Part 3 surveys some of the most recent innovative research on fallacies, and Part 4 considers some of the current research topics in fallacy theory.
6.3 Description SEP: Errors in reasoning or argumentation. The argument conception treats fallacies as deceptively bad arguments appearing valid; the belief conception treats them as false but popular beliefs. Common fallacies include ad hominem, straw man, appeal to authority, and hasty generalization, raising questions about argumentation norms and critical thinking.

6 Description GPT5: A fallacy in which an argument substitutes eliciting emotions (fear, pity, anger, pride, disgust) for providing relevant reasons or evidence for a conclusion. Emotional considerations can be relevant in practical or moral reasoning, but the fallacy arises when emotion is used as a stand-in for warranted support.
The transition from one or more propositions to another under rules or norms; includes deductive entailment and non-deductive support such as induction and abduction.
6.1 Description Gemini: The process of drawing conclusions or making judgments based on evidence and reasoning.

6 Description GPT5: Acquiring belief from others' assertions; raises questions about the division of epistemic labor, trust, social norms of assertion, and the conditions for testimonial knowledge.
6.1 Description Gemini: The words, gestures, or utterances from others that convey beliefs and information. In epistemology, it is a major source of knowledge about the world.

6 Description GPT5: Debates whether testimonial justification depends on independent evidence of reliability (reductionism) or can be basic given normal conditions (anti-reductionism), and how testimony transmits knowledge.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical study of how beliefs formed on the basis of testimony are justified and can count as knowledge. It examines the conditions under which we can trust what others tell us.

6 Description GPT5: Accounts of what counts as testimony-telling, asserting, or communicating with an audience-its constitutive norms (sincerity, competence), and the hearer's responsibilities.
6.1 Description Gemini: The inquiry into the fundamental characteristics of testimony as a source of information, including its dependence on the speaker's knowledge and sincerity.

6 Description GPT5: The specific statuses and conditions that tell you how a belief stands epistemically; whether it is knowledge or mere opinion, justified or unsupported, rational or irrational, warranted or defeated, and whether its truth is an achievement or just luck. They are the evaluative categories used to assess beliefs and inquiry: knowledge, justification, evidence, rationality, warrant, understanding, and wisdom, along with what counts as rational belief and responsible inquiry. They explain why some beliefs amount to genuine epistemic achievement (e.g., knowledge) while others are mere true belief, guesswork, or luck.  They are the diagnostic categories epistemologists use to explain why two people can share the same true belief while only one of them genuinely knows it
6.1 Description Gemini: The category of concepts that describe the cognitive and normative properties of a person's beliefs, such as whether a belief is justified, warranted, or constitutes knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: The relation between a belief and the consideration(s) that actually cause or sustain it in the believer; what the believer is relying on, as opposed to what they merely have available. A belief is properly based when the belief is formed and maintained because of the relevant evidence or reasons, not due to luck, habit, wishful thinking, or a different (poor) reason.
6.1 Description Gemini: The relationship between a belief and the grounds (reasons or evidence) that support it. A belief is properly based on evidence if the evidence is the reason the belief is held.

6 Description GPT5: Systematic distortion in how one searches for, interprets, or weighs evidence, often pushing belief away from what the evidence supports. It can create unwarranted confidence and resist correction even when counterevidence is available.

Information that removes or weakens a belief’s justification. A defeater either supports the opposite conclusion (rebutting defeat) or undermines the connection between your evidence and the conclusion (undercutting defeat).
6.1 Description Gemini: The loss of justification for a belief. A "defeater" is a new piece of evidence or a new belief that undermines or overrides one's justification for another belief.

6 Description GPT5: A non-evidential epistemic right or permission to accept certain fundamental commitments or sources (i.e., hinges, perception, memory, and often testimony) given our epistemic position, so long as no specific defeaters are present. It is invoked to explain how ordinary knowledge and rational belief can be possible without requiring an evidential proof of the reliability of these basic practices in every case.
6.1 Description Gemini: A form of epistemic justification or warrant that does not require the believer to have conscious access to the reasons supporting their belief. It is a right to believe, often default and unearned.

Statements about what might be the case given a specific body of information or evidence. A proposition is considered epistemically possible if it is not ruled out by what is currently known or assumed.

Situations arise where a subject forms a true belief in an accidental or coincidental manner, such as through a guess. Since knowledge requires a robust, non-accidental connection to truth, the presence of this fortuity typically prevents a true belief from qualifying as knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: What supports propositions-ranging from experiences, memories, and data to knowledge itself-subject to debates about its nature (propositional vs non-propositional) and possession.
6.1 Description Gemini: The information or reasons that support a belief or proposition. It is a central concept in determining whether a belief is justified or rational.
6.2 Description SEP: For my own part, I think that if one were looking for a single phrase to capture the stage to which philosophy has progressed, 'the study of evidence' would be a better choice than 'the study of language'. -A.J. Ayer, Philosophy in the Twentieth Century And when we try to define 'evidence' . we find it very difficult. -R.G. Collingwood, The Idea of History The concept of evidence is central to both epistemology and the philosophy of science. Of course, 'evidence' is hardly a philosopher's term of art: it is not only, or even primarily, philosophers who routinely speak of evidence, but also lawyers and judges, historians and scientists, investigative journalists and reporters, as well as the members of numerous other professions and ordinary folk in the course of everyday life. The concept of evidence would thus seem to be on firmer pre-theoretical ground than various other concepts which enjoy similarly central standing within philosophy. (Contrast, for example, the epistemologist's quasi-technical term 'epistemic justification'.) When one compares philosophical accounts of evidence with the way the concept is often employed in non-philosophical contexts, however, a tension soon emerges. Consider first the kinds of things which non-philosophers are apt to count as evidence. For the forensics expert, evidence might consist of fingerprints on a gun, a bloodied knife, or a semen-stained dress: evidence is, paradigmatically, the kind of thing which one might place in a plastic bag and label 'Exhibit A'. Thus, a criminal defense attorney might float the hypothesis that the evidence which seems to incriminate his client was planted by a corrupt law enforcement official or hope for it to be misplaced by a careless clerk. For an archaeologist, evidence is the sort of thing which one might dig up from the ground and carefully send back to one's laboratory for further analysis. Similarly, for the historian, evidence might consist of hitherto overlooked documents recently discovered in an archive or in an individual's personal library.[1] Reflection on examples such as these naturally suggests that evidence consists paradigmatically of physical objects, or perhaps, physical objects arranged in certain ways. For presumably, physical objects are the sort of thing which one might place in a plastic bag, dig up from the ground, send to a laboratory, or discover among the belongings of an individual of historical interest. However natural such a picture might be, it is at least somewhat difficult to reconcile with historically prominent philosophical accounts of the nature of evidence. Russell, the greatest empiricist of the first half of the twentieth century, tended to think of evidence as sense data, mental items of one's present consciousness with which one is immediately acquainted. In this, he stood squarely within the tradition of classical empiricism. Quine, the greatest empiricist of the second half of the century, maintained throughout his career that evidence consisted of the stimulation of one's sensory receptors.[2] The logical positivists held that whatever evidence there is for a given scientific theory is afforded by observation statements or 'protocol sentence', linguistic entities with suitably-restricted contents; the precise nature of the restrictions became a vigorously contested matter within the tradition itself.[3] According to one recent and influential study, one's evidence consists of the totality of propositions that one knows (Williamson 2000). According to another, one's evidence consists exclusively of one's current mental states (Conee and Feldman 2004). Within contemporary confirmation theory, a prominent version of Bayesianism is naturally understood as identifying one's evidence with those beliefs of which one is psychologically certain. Of course, the suggestion that one might place sense data, sensory receptor stimulations, known propositions, or one's current mental states in a plastic bag (or dig them up from the ground, or send them to a laboratory, or .) is of dubious intelligibility. From the perspective of much ordinary thought and talk about evidence, much philosophical theorizing about evidence would seem to embody a particularly grotesque category mistake. Moreover, it is not simply that the accounts of evidence that have been advanced by philosophers stand in at least some prima facie tension with much that is said and thought about evidence outside of philosophy. As even the cursory survey offered above makes clear, philosophers themselves have offered quite divergent theories of what sorts of things are eligible to serve as evidence. What might account for such discrepancies? One possibility is the following. Both in and outside of philosophy, the concept of evidence has often been called upon to fill a number of distinct roles. Although some of these roles are complementary, others stand in at least some measure of tension with one another. Indeed, as we will see below, it is far from obvious that any one thing could play all of the diverse roles that evidence has at various times been expected to play. Different theories about the nature of evidence might thus naturally emerge from different emphases on the competing demands that have been placed on the concept. In what follows, I survey some of the theoretical roles that the concept of evidence has been asked to play and explore some of the relations among them.
6.3 Description SEP: What justifies beliefs or supports hypotheses. Epistemological debates concern evidence's nature (mental states, propositions, or facts), whether evidence must be possessed or can be merely available, and relationships between evidence and justification. Bayesian approaches treat evidence as affecting probabilities; others emphasize evidential support relations.

6 Description GPT5: Explores whether knowledge is distinct from, grounded in, or identical to evidence (e.g., E=K), and how knowledge can serve as a normative basis for further belief.
6.1 Description Gemini: The relationship between having evidence for a proposition and having knowledge of that proposition. Traditionally, knowledge is considered to be, at minimum, a justified (evidence-based) true belief.

6 Description GPT5: The absence or limitation of knowledge-individual or collective-including varieties such as simple lack, closed-mindedness, and structurally produced ignorance within social systems.
6.1 Description Gemini: A state of lacking knowledge or information. Epistemologically, it is studied in relation to doubt, inquiry, and the limits of knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: The normative status that makes a belief epistemically appropriate, shaped by evidential support, reliability, coherence, and responsiveness to defeaters.
6.1 Description Gemini: The property of a belief being well-founded, supported by good reasons or evidence. It is a central requirement for a belief to count as knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: The problem of how reasons terminate without arbitrariness, motivating foundationalism, coherentism, infinitism, or hybrid accounts of structure.
6.1 Description Gemini: The problem that arises when one attempts to justify every belief. If every belief must be justified by another belief, this seems to lead to an infinite chain of justifications with no ultimate foundation.

A subject's belief enjoys positive status only if supported by the mental items or data currently in their possession. Two individuals with identical internal mental lives must therefore share the same degree of warrant for their views.

Cognitive structures ultimately rest on basic, self-evident beliefs that require no support from other propositions. These non-inferential starting points transfer positive status to derived views through logical connections.

An unlimited, non-repeating series of reasons supports a view, rejecting both circular reasoning and finite stopping points. Warrant arises from the availability of an endless chain of support for any challenged claim.

Propositional justification concerns possessing adequate reasons for a view, regardless of whether the agent actually accepts the conclusion. Doxastic requires that the agent holds the view specifically because of those valid reasons, establishing a proper causal connection.

6 Description GPT5: Conformity to norms governing belief and action-structural (coherence, consistency) and substantive (evidence-responsiveness, accuracy)-often linked to decision-theoretic ideals.
6.1 Description Gemini: The quality of being based on or in accordance with reason or logic. In epistemology, it refers to holding beliefs that are coherent and well-supported by evidence.

6 Description GPT5: Failures to meet rational norms, including akrasia, wishful thinking, motivated reasoning, or incoherence, sometimes explicable by cognitive biases or conflicts of aims.
6.1 Description Gemini: The state of holding beliefs that are contradictory, not based on evidence, or formed through faulty reasoning.

6 Description GPT5: Specific normative constraints on attitudes (e.g., means-ends coherence, closure under known implication, no contradictory beliefs) that guide rational deliberation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The principles or norms that govern rational thought and belief. These are the rules one ought to follow to be considered rational (e.g., believing the logical consequences of one's beliefs).

6 Description GPT5: A cognitive achievement involving grasp of explanatory, structural, or modal relations, often requiring more than true belief and allowing a degree of fallibility.
6.1 Description Gemini: A cognitive state of grasping or comprehending a subject, which is often considered a deeper epistemic achievement than merely knowing facts about it.
6.2 Description SEP: Understanding is a protean concept in philosophy, and the desire for understanding is pervasive in everyday life. Scientists take it as their goal to understand the world and how it works, teachers and parents hope to transmit understanding to their students and children, and from a political and social point of view we often strive for mutual understanding. This entry will look at some ways in which understanding has been conceptualized by philosophers-especially epistemologists, philosophers of science, and philosophers of social science. The focus will be on accounts of understanding the natural world and other people, and it will only touch occasionally on accounts of what it takes to understand other items, such as concepts, languages, or texts.
6.3 Description SEP: Check knowledge? Seeing the whole, grasping connections/causes (explanatory understanding). Objectual understanding (understanding a subject matter). Is understanding transparent (can't be lucky)? Kvanvig: Understanding, not knowledge, is the goal of inquiry.

6 Description GPT5: That which, when added to true belief, yields knowledge; on proper-function accounts, arises when cognitive faculties operate as designed in suitable environments.
6.1 Description Gemini: The property that, when added to true belief, is said to yield knowledge. What exactly constitutes warrant (e.g., justification, reliability) is a major topic of debate.

6 Description GPT5: Integrated excellence in judgment concerning ends and means, combining factual knowledge, practical insight, and normative discernment across domains.
6.1 Description Gemini: A deep and fundamental understanding of existence, life, and oneself, which combines knowledge with sound judgment and the ability to apply it practically.
6.2 Description SEP: What is wisdom? Philosophers, psychologists, spiritual leaders, poets, novelists, life coaches, and a variety of other important thinkers have tried to understand the concept of wisdom. This entry will provide a brief and general overview, and analysis of, several philosophical views on the topic of wisdom. It is not intended to capture the many interesting and important approaches to wisdom found in other fields of inquiry. Moreover, this entry will focus on several major ideas in the Western philosophical tradition. In particular, it will focus on five general approaches to understanding what it takes to be wise: (1) wisdom as epistemic humility, (2) wisdom as epistemic accuracy, (3) wisdom as knowledge, (4) a hybrid theory of wisdom, and (5) wisdom as rationality.
6.3 Description SEP: Phronesis (Practical Wisdom) vs. Sophia (Theoretical Wisdom). Epistemic humility (Socrates). Knowledge of how to live well (Stoics). Nozick: Wisdom is understanding what matters. Is it a skill or a virtue?

6 Description GPT5: Systematic accounts explaining knowledge and justification-foundational, coherentist, reliabilist, evidentialist, contextualist, virtue-theoretic, and hybrid approaches.
6.1 Description Gemini: The broad category of philosophical theories that attempt to explain the nature of knowledge, justification, and other key epistemic concepts.

6 Description GPT5: Theory that justification derives from mutual support within a system of beliefs, emphasizing coherence, explanatory integration, and absence of internal conflict.
6.1 Description Gemini: The theory that a belief is justified if it is part of a coherent system of beliefs. Justification is a function of how well a belief fits with other beliefs, rather than its grounding in a foundational base.

6 Description GPT5: View that certain experiences-especially perceptual presentations-confer immediate prima facie justification without antecedent argument, defeasible by counterevidence.
6.1 Description Gemini: An epistemological stance that asserts that some beliefs can be justified without being based on any evidence or reasons, particularly in the context of perception.

6 Description GPT5: Application of dogmatism to sensory experience: if it visually seems that p, one thereby has prima facie justification to believe p, absent defeaters.
6.1 Description Gemini: The specific application of dogmatism to perceptual beliefs, holding that if it seems to you that you see something, you are immediately justified in believing it is there, absent any defeaters.

4 Topic: Dogmatist and Moorean Replies to Skepticism
6 Description GPT5: Anti-skeptical strategies appealing to ordinary knowledge or immediate perceptual justification to resist skeptical hypotheses and closure-based arguments.
6.1 Description Gemini: A response to skepticism that relies on either common-sense, dogmatic assertions (like G.E. Moore's "Here is one hand") or a dogmatist theory of perception to block skeptical arguments.

6 Description GPT5: What counts as knowledge or justification is partly made/constructed by cognitive or social practices (concepts, inferential norms, methods, standards of evidence) rather than simply discovered. Treats standards of justification as fixed by the rules and methods of inquiry we adopt. Weaker versions: preserve mind-independent reality. Stronger versions: extend construction claims toward truth or reality itself.
Semantics on which "knows" varies with conversational context (stakes, alternatives), reconciling everyday knowledge claims with stricter philosophical demands.
6.1 Description Gemini: The view that the meaning of knowledge attributions (e.g., "S knows that p") depends on the context of the person making the attribution. The standards for what counts as knowledge can vary with conversational context.
6.2 Description SEP: Epistemic Contextualism (EC) is a recent and hotly debated position. EC is roughly the view that what is expressed by a knowledge attribution - a claim to the effect that S "knows" that p - depends partly on something in the context of the attributor, and hence the view is often called 'attributor contextualism'. Because such an utterance is context-dependent, so too is whether the attribution is true. The typical EC view identifies the pivotal contextual features as the attributor's practical stake in the truth of p, or the prominence in the attributor's situation of skeptical doubts about knowledge. The typical EC view has it that as the stakes rise or the skeptical doubts become more serious, the contextual standard gets more demanding. It requires S to be in a better position if the attributor's claim, "S knows that p", is to express a truth. In contrast, Invariantists about knowledge hold that such factors in the attributor's context do not affect the standards that must be met by a true "knowledge" attribution. In addition to marking an important departure from traditional epistemological assumptions, EC is claimed to provide a novel resolution to certain puzzles about knowledge-not least, skeptical ones-as well as to best comport with our everyday "knowledge"-attributing practices. What follows describes the leading forms of EC, so understood, as well as the principal arguments for and major objections to EC. Along the way, EC is situated with respect to certain other views, both kindred and competing.

6 Description GPT5: Explanations of how knowledge ascriptions can be true in low-stakes contexts yet falter under skeptical pressure, dissolving paradox without granting global ignorance.
6.1 Description Gemini: A specific application of contextualism that aims to solve the problem of skepticism by arguing that in ordinary contexts, we can truthfully say we know many things, while in skeptical contexts, the standards for knowledge become too high to meet.

6 Description GPT5: Comparison between context-sensitive accounts and invariantism, which holds fixed truth-conditions for "knows" across contexts, with debates over intuitions and linguistic data.
6.1 Description Gemini: The debate between contextualism, which holds that knowledge standards vary with context, and invariantism, which holds that the standards for knowledge are fixed and do not change.

6 Description GPT5: Contrasts contextualism with relativism, where truth depends on parameters beyond context of utterance (e.g., assessor), assessing explanatory payoffs and costs.
6.1 Description Gemini: The intersection between contextualism and relativism, exploring whether the context-sensitivity of knowledge claims implies that truth itself is relative to a particular framework or perspective.

6 Description GPT5: Treats knowledge as a three-place relation-knowing p rather than q-making "contrast classes" central to explanation, skepticism, and practical reasoning.
6.1 Description Gemini: The theory that knowledge is a three-part relation between a subject, a proposition, and a contrasting proposition. To know that 'p' is to know that 'p' rather than 'q'.

6 Description GPT5: One can have knowledge or justified belief even though their evidence and reasoning do not guarantee truth and they could be mistaken. It rejects the idea that certainty or infallible justification is required for knowledge. Justification is sufficient when it is strong and appropriate, not when it is certainty-producing
6.1 Description Gemini: View that absolute certainty is not a prerequisite for knowledge. It holds that an agent can possess knowledge of a proposition even if their justification does not logically guarantee its truth, meaning it is possible to know something yet still theoretically be mistaken.

6 Description GPT5: Knowledge requires error-excluding support: if you know, your grounds must guarantee truth (or otherwise rule out the relevant possibility of being mistaken).
6.1 Description Gemini: Knowledge requires absolute certainty - the justification must be so strong that the possibility of error is logically excluded.

6 Description GPT5: Dispute over whether justificatory factors must be cognitively accessible to the subject (internalism) or may include mind-external, truth-conducive conditions (externalism).
6.1 Description Gemini: The debate concerning whether the justifying factors for a belief must be internal to the believer's mind (internalism) or can be external factors, such as the reliability of the belief-forming process (externalism).

6 Description GPT5: Holds that standards of truth or justification vary with frameworks or cultures. There are no absolute principles regarding normative belief, justification, or rationality, and that there are only relative ones.
6.1 Description Gemini: Knowledge, truth, or justification are relative to the standards of a particular culture, society, or individual. There are no absolute, universal standards.

6 Description GPT5: Truth, correctness, or appropriateness of key epistemic claims (esp. knowledge ascriptions like "S knows that p") depends in systematic ways on context or perspective. Contextualism typically ties this dependence to features of the attributor's conversational context (e.g., which error-possibilities are salient), while relativism treats epistemic evaluation as relative to a standpoint, assessor, or parameter that can vary across evaluators.

Epistemic status is fundamentally person-relative: what one is justified in believing (or what counts as "rational" or "correct" belief) is determined by the subject's own perspective, standards, or attitudes rather than by standpoint-independent epistemic norms. In contrast, objectivist views: treat evidence and rationality as binding in the same way across subjects.
6.1 Description Gemini: The position that the validity of epistemic norms or justification depends entirely on the individual agent's perspective, mental states, or community conventions. It denies the existence of objective, universal epistemic facts, implying that what counts as "rational" can vary fundamentally from person to person without an external standard to adjudicate.

6 Description GPT5: Treats epistemic status as person-relative (or attitude-dependent) rather than governed by shared evidence-sensitive norms. Used to block criticism by implying there is no standpoint-independent standard for assessing the claim beyond the speaker's attitude

Epistemic Empiricism: sensory experience is the primary source of forming ideas, knowledge and justifications (vs. innate ideas, traditions). Historically contrasted with rationalist appeals to a priori insight.
6.1 Description Gemini: Epistemological theory that all knowledge is derived from sensory experience. Emphasizes the role of empirical evidence in the formation of ideas.

6 Description GPT5: Maintains that what one ought to believe is wholly determined by one's evidence and its support relations, independent of practical or moral stakes.
6.1 Description Gemini: The view that a belief is justified if and only if it is supported by the believer's evidence. The strength of the justification is proportional to the strength of the evidence.

Natural selection drives the development of cognitive mechanisms, ensuring a functional fit between an organism's apparatus and the world. It applies evolutionary models to the growth of scientific theories, viewing knowledge progress as a selection process similar to biological adaptation.

6 Description GPT5: Structural view that justificatory chains terminate in basic beliefs warranted non-inferentially (e.g., by perception or introspection) which support all other justified beliefs.
6.1 Description Gemini: The theory that all justified beliefs ultimately rest upon a foundation of non-inferential, basic beliefs that are justified without reference to other beliefs.

6 Description GPT5: Examines tensions and hybrids between foundational structure and holistic coherence, including how basing and defeat operate in each.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical debate and exploration of hybrid theories that combine elements of both foundationalism (basic, self-justifying beliefs) and coherentism (justification through mutual support).

6 Description GPT5: Proposes that justification can consist in an endless, non-repeating chain of reasons, avoiding arbitrariness without circularity or foundations.
6.1 Description Gemini: A theory of justification that rejects foundationalism by holding that a belief can be justified by an infinite chain of reasons.

Practical or moral factors can affect whether a subject knows a proposition or is justified in believing it. The stakes of being wrong or the moral consequences of a belief can raise the threshold for the evidence required to possess knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: Claims that substantive knowledge about the world can be obtained independently of sense experience, typically via reason, reflection, or intellectual insight.
6.1 Description Gemini: Reason is the primary source of knowledge and justification, often contrasted with empiricism. It asserts that some knowledge is attainable a priori.

6 Description GPT5: Ties justification or knowledge to the reliability of the processes that produce beliefs (e.g., perception, memory), allowing externalist justification.
6.1 Description Gemini: A family of theories that define justification or knowledge in terms of the reliability of the cognitive process that produces the belief.
6.3 Description SEP: The epistemological theory that justification or knowledge requires beliefs be formed by reliable processesa those tending to produce true beliefs. Process reliabilism addresses the Gettier problem and regress of justification. Challenges include the generality problem (individuating processes) and whether reliability suffices for justification without awareness.

6 Description GPT5: Asserts that a true belief counts as knowledge when produced by a reliable cognitive process functioning properly in appropriate conditions.
6.1 Description Gemini: The specific application of reliabilism to knowledge, where knowledge is defined as a true belief formed by a reliable cognitive process.

6 Description GPT5: Holds that a belief is justified to the extent it is formed by a process that tends to produce true beliefs in relevantly similar circumstances.
6.1 Description Gemini: The specific application of reliabilism to justification, where a belief is justified if it is the result of a reliable cognitive process.

6 Description GPT5: Challenge of individuating the relevant process type for reliability assessment without triviality, since any token process belongs to many types.
6.1 Description Gemini: A major objection to reliabilism, which states that it is difficult to specify the precise "type" of cognitive process that is supposed to be reliable. A process can be described broadly (e.g., "vision") or narrowly (e.g., "vision in foggy conditions"), yielding different reliability assessments.

6 Description GPT5: Approach locating knowledge/justification in the exercise of intellectual virtues (e.g., open-mindedness, intellectual courage), often modeling knowledge as apt or creditable success.
6.1 Description Gemini: An approach to epistemology that emphasizes the importance of intellectual virtues (e.g., open-mindedness, intellectual courage) in the acquisition and justification of knowledge.
6.2 Description SEP: Contemporary virtue epistemology (hereafter 'VE') is a diverse collection of approaches to epistemology. At least two central tendencies are discernible among the approaches. First, they view epistemology as a normative discipline. Second, they view intellectual agents and communities as the primary focus of epistemic evaluation, with a focus on the intellectual virtues and vices embodied in and expressed by these agents and communities. This entry introduces many of the most important results of the contemporary VE research program. These include novel attempts to resolve longstanding disputes, solve perennial problems, grapple with novel challenges, and expand epistemology's horizons. In the process, it reveals the diversity within VE. Beyond sharing the two unifying commitments mentioned above, its practitioners diverge over the nature of intellectual virtues, which questions to ask, and which methods to use. It will be helpful to note some terminology before proceeding. First, we use 'cognitive', 'epistemic' and 'intellectual' synonymously. Second, we often use 'normative' broadly to include not only norms and rules, but also duties and values. Finally, 'practitioners' names contemporary virtue epistemologists.

6.1 Description Gemini: Distinct areas of inquiry (i.e., religion, mathematics, or morality) may require unique epistemic standards or methods. This approach investigates whether knowledge in these fields relies on domain-specific faculties like intuition, revelation, or specialized forms of reasoning.

Riddles involving knowledge or justification expose deep inconsistencies in our epistemic concepts and intuitions. These puzzles, such as the Surprise Test or the Knowability Paradox, often force a revision of principles like deductive closure or the nature of evidence.
6.2 Description SEP: Epistemic paradoxes are riddles that turn on the concept of knowledge (episteme is Greek for knowledge). Typically, there are conflicting, well-credentialed answers to these questions (or pseudo-questions). Thus the riddle immediately informs us of an inconsistency. In the long run, the riddle goads and guides us into correcting at least one deep error - if not directly about knowledge, then about its kindred concepts such as justification, rational belief, and evidence. Such corrections are of interest to epistemologists. Historians date the origin of epistemology to the appearance of skeptics. As manifest in Plato's dialogues featuring Socrates, epistemic paradoxes have been discussed for twenty five hundred years. Given their hardiness, some of these riddles about knowledge will be discussed for the next twenty five hundred years.
6.3 Description SEP: Puzzles about knowledge including the surprise examination paradox, the knowability paradox, and Fitch's paradox. These reveal tensions between plausible epistemic principles. The knowability paradox suggests that if all truths are knowable, then all truths are known. Such paradoxes illuminate knowledge's logic and limits.

6.1 Description Gemini: A rational person can seemingly be justified in believing that any single ticket in a fair lottery will lose, yet cannot rationally believe that all tickets will lose. This puzzle challenges the idea that high probability alone is sufficient for justification or that justification is preserved through conjunction.

Knowability (Fitch's Paradox): The thesis that "all truths are knowable" logically entails the much stronger and implausible claim that "all truths are actually known." This paradox threatens anti-realist views that attempt to define truth in terms of possible verification or epistemic accessibility.

A self-referential sentence stating "This sentence is not known by [Subject]" creates a contradiction if we assume basic principles about knowledge and truth. It demonstrates the difficulties that arise when epistemic concepts are treated as predicates within a formal language that allows self-reference.

Ignorance is not merely a void or lack of knowledge but often a substantive, socially constructed practice that sustains power structures. It investigates how dominant groups actively produce or maintain gaps in understanding to preserve privilege.

6 Description GPT5: Knowledge growth is an evolutionary process: variation in conjectures or representations, selective filtering by criticism, evidence, and environmental constraints, and retention of "fit" candidates. Inquiry progresses less by direct access to foundations than by iterated selection among competing hypotheses or strategies.
6.2 Description SEP: Evolutionary Epistemology is a naturalistic approach to epistemology which emphasizes the importance of natural selection in two primary roles. In the first role, selection is the generator and maintainer of the reliability of our senses and cognitive mechanisms, as well as the "fit" between those mechanisms and the world. In the second role, trial and error learning and the evolution of scientific theories are construed as selection processes.

6.1 Description Gemini: The concepts, standards, and objects of knowledge themselves have a history and change over time. It examines how epistemic categories like "objectivity" or "observation" are not timeless universals but emerge from specific historical and cultural practices.

Inquiry into knowledge should be continuous with, or even replaced by, the methods and results of the natural sciences like psychology and cognitive science. It rejects the idea of a distinct, a priori "first philosophy" in favor of studying how human subjects actually form beliefs and theories.

6 Description GPT5: Studies the "oughts" and "maybes" governing belief, credence, inquiry, and assertion-i.e., what it is rationally required, permitted, or forbidden to think given one's evidence and cognitive aims. It asks what grounds these epistemic standards (truth, knowledge, understanding, accuracy, or intellectual virtue) and how they relate to practical and moral reasons
6.1 Description Gemini: Study of the normative status of beliefs, questioning what makes a belief "right," "rational," or "justified." It examines the rules and obligations-such as the duty to believe the truth-that govern our intellectual lives.

6 Description GPT5: We have voluntary control over at least some of our beliefs (or belief-like attitudes), either directly (at will) or indirectly (through managing evidence, attention, and inquiry). Control is a precondition for epistemic responsibility and blame.
6.1 Description Gemini: Humans have voluntary control over their beliefs, akin to the control of their actions. Aks whether we can choose to believe a proposition at will or if belief is an involuntary response to evidence

6 Description GPT5: Pertaining to belief (from doxa, "opinion" or "belief"). In epistemology, a "doxastic attitude" is an attitude toward a proposition (i.e., believing it, disbelieving it, suspending judgment about it, or having a certain degree of confidence in it).

Standards that govern what it is rational or permissible to believe, how confident to be, and how to update or inquire given evidence (e.g., coherence, evidential fit, and good updating). They are "epistemic" insofar as they aim at truth, knowledge, understanding, or accuracy rather than merely practical success.
6.1 Description Gemini: Standards that govern correct belief formation and maintenance. These norms dictate that beliefs should be formed based on sufficient evidence, truth, and logical consistency rather than wishful thinking or pragmatic utility.

6 Description GPT5: The same total body of evidence can make more than one doxastic attitude rationally permissible (e.g., two people with the same evidence may each be justified in adopting different credences, or in believing versus suspending judgment). In contrast, the "Uniqueness Thesis" holds: evidence always necessitates one specific rational response.
6.1 Description Gemini: A single body of total evidence can rationally support more than one unique doxastic attitude toward a proposition. This stands in contrast to the "Uniqueness Thesis," which argues that evidence always necessitates one specific rational response.

6 Description GPT5: Managing beliefs and inquiries Responsibly in ways that meet reasonable standards of intellectual care (i.e., seeking and weighing evidence, updating when warranted, checking bias, and deferring to expertise when appropriate). Epistemic Irresponsibility: avoidable cognitive failures (i.e., negligent inquiry, willful ignorance, careless acceptance of testimony, or motivated resistance to counterevidence).
6.1 Description Gemini: Obligation to exercise proper care, diligence, and scrutiny in their inquiry and belief-forming practices. Agents are accountable for their beliefs and can be praised or blamed based on their intellectual conduct.

6 Description GPT5: The set of goods that make epistemic states, practices, or methods worth having, such as truth, knowledge, understanding, explanatory power, and accuracy (aka. "epistemic utility"). Debates focus on which values are fundamental and how they justify epistemic norms.
6.1 Description Gemini: What makes certain cognitive states (i.e., knowledge or understanding) good or desirable. It addresses the "value problem," asking why knowledge is more valuable than mere true belief, and distinguishes between instrumental value (usefulness) and final value (good in itself).

6 Description GPT5: Excellent cognitive or intellectual character that tend to produce epistemic goods-either as reliable competences (e.g., perceptual acuity, good inference) or as responsible traits (e.g., open-mindedness, conscientiousness, intellectual humility). Virtue epistemology shifts the focus of analysis from the properties of individual beliefs to the qualities of the agent forming them.
6.1 Description Gemini: Excellent intellectual character traits (open-mindedness, intellectual humility, conscientiousness) that help agents reliably reach the truth. Virtue epistemology shifts the focus of analysis from the properties of individual beliefs to the qualities of the agent forming them.

Non-empirical criteria used to evaluate and select competing scientific or philosophical theories. Key Virtues: simplicity (parsimony), explanatory power, internal consistency / coherence, and fertility / fruitfulness (able to generate new predictions). These virtues guide theory choice when data underdetermine which theory to accept.

6 Description GPT5: When it is permissible, obligatory, or wrong to form, hold, or express beliefs, especially in relation to evidence and inquiry duties. Classic disputes contrast strict evidence-first (W.K. Clifford) positions [evidentialism] with views allowing belief beyond evidence (William James) in certain practical or existential contexts [pragmatist].
6.1 Description Gemini: Examines the moral obligations surrounding belief formation. Classicaly, it involves the debate of W.K. Clifford's evidentialism (it is wrong to believe on insufficient evidence) and William James's pragmatist defense of the right to believe in certain contexts.
6.2 Description SEP: The "ethics of belief" refers to a cluster of questions at the intersection of epistemology, ethics, philosophy of mind, and psychology. The central question in the debate is whether there are norms of some sort governing our habits of belief-formation, belief-maintenance, and belief-relinquishment. Is it ever or always morally wrong (or epistemically irrational, or practically imprudent) to hold a belief on insufficient evidence? Is it ever or always morally right (or epistemically rational, or practically prudent) to believe on the basis of sufficient evidence, or to withhold belief in the perceived absence of it? Is it ever or always obligatory to seek out all available epistemic evidence for a belief? Are there some ways of obtaining evidence that are themselves immoral, irrational, imprudent? Related questions have to do with the nature and structure of the norms involved, if any, as well as the source of their authority. Are they instrumental norms grounded in contingent ends that we set for ourselves? Are they categorical norms grounded in ends set for us by the very nature of our intellectual or moral capacities? Are there other options? And what are the objects of evaluation in this context-believers, beliefs, or both? Finally, assuming that there are norms of some sort governing belief-formation, what does that imply about the nature of belief? Does it imply that belief-formation is voluntary or under our control? If so, what sort of control is this? If not, then is talk of an ethics of belief even coherent?

6 Description GPT5: Principles stating when a speaker is entitled to assert a proposition (e.g., only if one knows it, only if one is justified, or only if it is true and properly grounded). These norms connect epistemic status to the social act of putting information into circulation for others to rely on.
6.1 Description Gemini: Rules governing the speech act of assertion, defining what condition one must meet to properly assert a proposition p. The dominant view is the Knowledge Norm of Assertion, which states that one must know p in order to legitimately assert it.

6 Description GPT5: Epistemic deontologism: a normative theory of what it is for belief to be justified or permissible.

6.2 Description SEP: Formal epistemology explores knowledge and reasoning using "formal" tools, tools from math and logic. For example, a formal epistemologist might use probability theory to explain how scientific reasoning works. Or she might use modal logic to defend a particular theory of knowledge. The questions that drive formal epistemology are often the same as those that drive "informal" epistemology. What is knowledge, and how is it different from mere opinion? What separates science from pseudoscience? When is a belief justified? What justifies my belief that the sun will rise tomorrow, or that the external world is real and not an illusion induced by Descartes' demon? And yet, the tools formal epistemologists apply to these questions share much history and interest with other fields, both inside and outside philosophy. So formal epistemologists often ask questions that aren't part of the usual epistemological core, questions about decision-making ( 5.1) or the meaning of hypothetical language ( 5.3), for example. Perhaps the best way to get a feel for formal epistemology is to look at concrete examples. We'll take a few classic epistemological questions and look at popular formal approaches to them, to see what formal tools bring to the table. We'll also look at some applications of these formal methods outside epistemology.

A proposition A is mutual knowledge among a set of agents if each agent knows that A. Mutual knowledge by itself implies nothing about what, if any, knowledge anyone attributes to anyone else. Suppose each student arrives for a class meeting knowing that the instructor will be late. That the instructor will be late is mutual knowledge, but each student might think only she knows the instructor will be late. However, if one of the students says openly "Peter told me he will be late again," then each student knows that each student knows that the instructor will be late, each student knows that each student knows that each student knows that the instructor will be late, and so on, ad infinitum. The announcement made the mutually known fact common knowledge among the students. Common knowledge is a phenomenon which underwrites much of social life. In order to communicate or otherwise coordinate their behavior successfully, individuals typically require mutual or common understandings or background knowledge. Indeed, if a particular interaction results in "failure", the usual explanation for this is that the agents involved did not have the common knowledge that would have resulted in success. If a married couple are separated in a department store, they stand a good chance of finding one another because their common knowledge of each others' tastes and experiences leads them each to look for the other in a part of the store both know that both would tend to frequent. Since the spouses both love cappuccino, each expects the other to go to the coffee bar, and they find one another. But in a less happy case, if a pedestrian causes a minor traffic jam by crossing against a red light, she explains her mistake as the result of her not noticing, and therefore not knowing, the status of the traffic signal that all the motorists knew. The spouses coordinate successfully given their common knowledge, while the pedestrian and the motorists miscoordinate as the result of a breakdown in common knowledge. Given the importance of common knowledge in social interactions, it is remarkable that only quite recently have philosophers and social scientists attempted to analyze the concept. David Hume (1740) was perhaps the first to make explicit reference to the role of mutual knowledge in coordination. In his account of convention in A Treatise of Human Nature, Hume argued that a necessary condition for coordinated activity was that agents all know what behavior to expect from one another. Without the requisite mutual knowledge, Hume maintained, mutually beneficial social conventions would disappear. Much later, J. E. Littlewood (1953) presented some examples of common-knowledge-type reasoning, and Thomas Schelling (1960) and John Harsanyi (1967-1968) argued that something like common knowledge is needed to explain certain inferences people make about each other. The philosopher Robert Nozick describes, but does not develop, the notion in his doctoral dissertation (Nozick 1963), while the first mathematical analysis and application of the notion of common knowledge is found in the technical report by Friedell (1967), then published as (Friedell 1969).[1] The first full-fledged philosophical analysis of common knowledge was offered by David Lewis (1969) in the monograph Convention. Stephen Schiffer (1972), Robert Aumann (1976), and Gilbert Harman (1977) independently gave alternate definitions of common knowledge. Jon Barwise (1988, 1989) gave a precise formulation of Harman's intuitive account. Throughout the 1980's a number of epistemic logicians, both from philosophy and from computer science, studied the logical structure of common knowledge, and the interested reader should consult the relevant portions of the two important monographs (Fagin et al. 1995) and (Meyer and Van der Hoek 1995). Margaret Gilbert (1989) proposed a somewhat different account of common knowledge which she argues is preferable to the standard account. Others have developed accounts of mutual knowledge, approximate common knowledge, and common belief which require less stringent assumptions than the standard account, and which serve as more plausible models of what agents know in cases where strict common knowledge seems impossible (Brandenburger and Dekel 1987, Stinchcombe 1988, Monderer and Samet 1989, Rubinstein 1992). The analysis and applications of common knowledge and related multi-agent knowledge concepts has become a lively field of research. The purpose of this essay is to overview of some of the most important results stemming from this contemporary research. The topics reviewed in each section of this essay are as follows: Section 1 gives motivating examples which illustrate a variety of ways in which the actions of agents depend crucially upon their having, or lacking, certain common knowledge. Section 2 discusses alternative analyses of common knowledge. Section 3 reviews applications of multi-agent knowledge concepts, particularly to game theory (von Neumann and Morgenstern 1944), in which common knowledge assumptions have been found to have great importance in justifying solution concepts for mathematical games. Section 4 discusses skeptical doubts about the attainability of common knowledge. Finally, Section 5 discusses the common belief concept which result from weakening the assumptions of Lewis' account of common knowledge.
6.3 Description SEP: A proposition is common knowledge when everyone knows it, everyone knows everyone knows it, and so on infinitely. Stronger than mutual knowledge, it requires each agent to attribute knowledge to others at all levels. Common knowledge is crucial for coordination, conventions, and game theory, raising questions about its achievability and epistemic logic.

Epistemology using formal methods from logic, probability theory, and decision theory. Topics include Bayesian confirmation, belief revision, epistemic logic, formal learning theory, and decision theory. Formal epistemology addresses precision in epistemological theorizing and connections between traditional and formal approaches.

6.2 Description SEP: In introductory classes to epistemology, we are taught to distinguish between three different kinds of knowledge. The first kind is acquaintance knowledge: we know our mothers, our friends, our pets, etc., by being acquainted with them. The second kind is knowledge of facts, propositional knowledge, or knowledge-that: this is the sort of knowledge we acquire when we learn that, say, Ithaca is in New York State or that Turin is located in Italy. It is customary to add to the list a third kind of knowledge that is supposed to be distinct both from acquaintance knowledge and from propositional knowledge. One possesses this knowledge when one can be truly described as knowing how to do something: play the piano, make a pie, walk, speak, create, build, and so on. The distinction between knowledge-how and knowledge-that was brought to scrutiny in analytic philosophy by Ryle in his seminal The Concept of Mind (1949), where he raised some of the now classical objections to the so-called "intellectualist legend": the view that knowledge-how amounts to knowledge-that. Ryle instead advocated an "anti-intellectualist" view of knowledge-how according to which knowledge-how and knowledge-that are distinct kinds of knowledge, and manifestations of knowledge-how are not necessarily manifestations of knowledge-that. This anti-intellectualism has been the received view among philosophers for a long time. Even psychologists and neuroscientists have explicitly appealed to Ryle's classical distinction when discussing their empirical findings (e.g., Cohen & Squire 1980; Anderson 1983). Nevertheless, in the last twenty years, a renewed interest by epistemologists in the nature of knowledge-how has brought new life to the debate, where new versions of intellectualism and anti-intellectualism have been developed and argued for. The debate is partly epistemological: is knowledge-how an altogether distinct kind of knowledge, different from knowledge-that? But it is also about a psychological question: what kind of psychological state is knowledge-how? The goal of this entry is to overview the debate between intellectualists and anti-intellectualists, while highlighting the implications of this debate for related questions concerning intelligence, cognition, language, and skills. This entry starts by looking at some classical arguments against intellectualism about knowledge-how: the regress argument (section 1), the insufficiency argument (section 2), and the gradability argument (section 3). Then two motivating arguments for intellectualism are considered: the linguistic argument (section 4) and the action theory argument (section 5). Section 6 overviews the recent epistemological debate on whether knowledge-how and propositional knowledge have the same epistemic profile. Section 7 discusses the cognitive science argument against intellectualism. Section 8 surveys what forms anti-intellectualism about knowledge-how has taken in the recent literature. Section 9 looks at the relation between knowledge-how and skills. Section 10 discusses knowledge-how and other related topics.
6.3 Description SEP: Practical knowledge or know-how, as distinct from propositional knowledge (knowing-that) and acquaintance knowledge. Questions concern whether knowing how to I reduces to knowing that p (intellectualism) or constitutes irreducible ability. Gilbert Ryle distinguished knowing-how from knowing-that; Stanley and Williamson defend intellectualism. Debates address skill, understanding, and procedural knowledge.

6.2 Description SEP: Philosophical skepticism is interesting because there are intriguing arguments for it despite its initial implausibility. Many contemporary epistemological positions can be fruitfully presented as responding to some aspect of those arguments. For example, questions regarding principles of epistemic closure and transmission are closely related to the discussion of what we will call Cartesian Skepticism, as are views according to which we are entitled to dismiss skeptical hypotheses even though we do not have evidence against them. The traditional issue of the structure of knowledge and justification, engendering Foundationalism, Coherentism, and Infinitism, can be seen as resulting from one main argument for what we will call Pyrrhonian Skepticism. In what follows we present these two forms of skepticism and assess the main arguments for them.
6.3 Description SEP: The philosophical position questioning the possibility of certain knowledge across various domains. Skeptical arguments, despite their initial implausibility, raise fundamental challenges to our epistemic practices and have shaped responses in contemporary epistemology. These include closure principles, contextualism, and the possibility of dismissing skeptical hypotheses without evidence.

4 Topic: Dogmatist and Moorean Replies to Skepticism *

6.2 Description SEP: Until recently, epistemology-the study of knowledge and justified belief-was heavily individualistic in focus. The emphasis was on evaluating doxastic attitudes (beliefs and disbeliefs) of individuals in abstraction from their social environment. Social epistemology seeks to redress this imbalance by investigating the epistemic effects of social interactions and social systems. After giving an introduction, and reviewing the history of the field in sections 1 and 3, we move on to discuss central topics in social epistemology in section 3. These include testimony, peer disagreement, and judgment aggregation, among others. Section 4 turns to recent approaches which have used formal methods to address core topics in social epistemology, as well as wider questions about the functioning of epistemic communities like those in science. In section 5 we briefly turn to questions related to social epistemology and the proper functioning of democratic societies.

We often find ourselves in disagreement with others. You may think nuclear energy is so volatile that no nuclear energy plants should be built anytime soon. But you are aware that there are many people who disagree with you on that very question. You disagree with your sister regarding the location of the piano in your childhood home, with you thinking it was in the primary living area and her thinking it was in the small den. You and many others believe Jesus Christ rose from the dead; millions of others disagree. It seems that awareness of disagreement can, at least in many cases, supply one with a powerful reason to think that one's belief is false. When you learned that your sister thought the piano had been in the den instead of the living room, you acquired a good reason to think it really wasn't in the living room, as you know full well that your sister is a generally intelligent individual, has the appropriate background experience (she lived in the house too), and is about as honest, forthright, and good at remembering events from childhood as you are. If, in the face of all this, you stick with your belief that the piano was in the living room, will your retaining that belief be reasonable? In the piano case there is probably nothing important riding on the question of what to do in the face of disagreement. But in many cases our disagreements are of great weight, both in the public arena and in our personal lives. You may disagree with your spouse or partner about whether to live together, whether to get married, where you should live, or how to raise your children. People with political power disagree about how to spend enormous amounts of money, or about what laws to pass, or about wars to fight. If only we were better able to resolve our disagreements, we would probably save millions of lives and prevent millions of others from living in poverty. This article examines the central epistemological issues tied to the recognition of disagreement. Compared to many other topics treated in this encyclopedia, the epistemology of disagreement is a mere infant. While the discussion of disagreement isn't altogether absent from the history of philosophy, philosophers didn't start, as a group, thinking about the topic in a rigorous and detailed way until the 21st century. For that reason, it is difficult to know what the primary issues and questions are concerning the general topic. At this early stage of investigation we are just getting our feet wet. In this essay, we begin by trying to motivate what we think should be the primary issues and questions before we move on to look at some of the main ideas in the literature. In so doing we also introduce some new terminology and make some novel distinctions that we think are helpful in navigating this relatively recent debate.
6.3 Description SEP: When individuals hold incompatible beliefs about the same question. Philosophical questions concern whether discovering peer disagreement should lead to revising beliefs, what epistemic significance disagreement has, and whether some disagreements are faultless. Debates address disagreement's implications for knowledge, rationality, and truth.

6 Description GPT5: Socially and informationally insulated "local worlds" in which groups stabilize their own standards of credibility, evidence, and salience, producing divergent pictures of what is real. The concept is used to explain belief polarization and persistent disagreement by highlighting how trust networks, media environments, and identity-driven interpretation shape what counts as a fact.
6.1 Description Gemini: The structural endpoint of polarization, where groups drift so far apart that they no longer share a common set of facts or "hinge propositions" (Deep Disagreement)

6 Description GPT5: Form of shared intentionality-joint beliefs, intentions, or plans-that enables coordinated action and the emergence of groups and institutions with irreducible social properties.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical study of shared mental states, such as joint intentions, shared beliefs, and collective emotions, which are considered foundational to social groups and institutions.

6 Description GPT5: Systematic inquiry into the most general features of reality-what there is and how it is structured-including categories (substance, property, event), dependence relations, modality, causation, space-time, and laws of nature.
6.1 Description Gemini: The branch of philosophy that studies the fundamental nature of reality, including the first principles of being, identity and change, space and time, causality, necessity, and possibility.
6.2 Description SEP: It is not easy to say what metaphysics is. Ancient and Medieval philosophers might have said that metaphysics was, like chemistry or astrology, to be defined by its subject-matter: metaphysics was the "science" that studied "being as such" or "the first causes of things" or "things that do not change". It is no longer possible to define metaphysics that way, for two reasons. First, a philosopher who denied the existence of those things that had once been seen as constituting the subject-matter of metaphysics-first causes or unchanging things-would now be considered to be making thereby a metaphysical assertion. Second, there are many philosophical problems that are now considered to be metaphysical problems (or at least partly metaphysical problems) that are in no way related to first causes or unchanging things-the problem of free will, for example, or the problem of the mental and the physical. The first three sections of this entry examine a broad selection of problems considered to be metaphysical and discuss ways in which the purview of metaphysics has expanded over time. We shall see that the central problems of metaphysics were significantly more unified in the Ancient and Medieval eras. Which raises a question-is there any common feature that unites the problems of contemporary metaphysics? The final two sections discuss some recent theories of the nature and methodology of metaphysics. We will also consider arguments that metaphysics, however defined, is an impossible enterprise.
6.3 Description SEP: Study of the fundamental nature of reality, existence, and being. Topics include ontology (what exists), modality (possibility), time, space, causation, and identity. Metaphysics investigates the structure of reality beyond physics. Critiques (Kant, Logical Positivism) question its possibility.

6 Description GPT5: Competing analyses of causation-counterfactual dependence, processes, mechanisms, probabilistic and productive relations-and their implications for agency and explanation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical investigation into the fundamental nature of the causal relation itself.
