An analogy with sense experience is helpful here. If a subject feels a general feeling of happiness, not on account of anything in particular, and later comes to believe the feeling was caused by the presence of a particular person, that fact does not transform the feeling of happiness into a perception of the person. Just as a mental event, to be a perception of an object, must in some sense seem to be an experience of that object, a religiously oriented mental event, to be a religious experience, must in some way seem to be an experience of a religiously significant reality. So, although religious feelings may be involved in many, or even most, religious experiences, they are not the same thing. Discussions of religious experience in terms of feelings, like Schleiermacher's (1998) "feeling of absolute dependence," or Otto's (1923) feeling of the numinous, were important early contributions to theorizing about religious experience, but some have since then argued (see Gellman 2001 and Alston 1991, for example) that religious affective states are not all there is to religious experience. To account for the experiences qua experiences, we must go beyond subjective feelings. Religious experience is also to be distinguished from mystical experience. Although there is obviously a close connection between the two, and mystical experiences are religious experiences, not all religious experiences qualify as mystical. The word 'mysticism' has been understood in many different ways. James (1902) took mysticism to necessarily involve ineffability, which would rule out many cases commonly understood to be mystical. Alston (1991) adopted the term grudgingly as the best of a bad lot and gave it a semi-technical meaning. But in its common, non-technical sense, mysticism is a specific religious system or practice, deliberately undertaken in order to come to some realization or insight, to come to unity with the divine, or to experience the ultimate reality directly. At the very least, religious experiences form a broader category; many religious experiences, like those of Saint Paul, Arjuna, Moses, Muhammad, and many others come unsought, not as the result of some deliberate practice undertaken to produce an experience.
6.3 Description SEP: Direct experience of the divine (mysticism, visions). Argument from Religious Experience (Swinburne): principle of credulitya if it seems God is present, probably He is. Explanations: neurological (temporal lobe), psychological (projection), or veridical contact.

6.2 Description SEP: A Trinity doctrine is commonly expressed as the statement that the one God exists as or in three equally divine "Persons", the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Every term in this statement (God, exists, as or in, equally divine, Person) has been variously understood. The guiding principle has been the creedal declaration that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit of the New Testament are consubstantial (i.e. the same in substance or essence, Greek: homoousios). Because this shared substance or essence is a divine one, this is understood to imply that all three named individuals are divine, and equally so. Yet the three in some sense "are" the one God of the Bible. After its formulation and imperial enforcement towards the end of the fourth century, this sort of Christian theology reigned more or less unchallenged. But before this, and again in post-Reformation modernity, the origin, meaning, and justification of trinitarian doctrine has been repeatedly disputed. These debates are discussed in supplementary documents to this entry. One aspect of these debates concerns the self-consistency of trinitarian theology. If there are three who are equally divine, isn't that to say there are at least three gods? Yet the tradition asserts exactly one god. Is the tradition, then, incoherent, and so self-refuting? Since the revival of analytic philosophy of religion in the 1960s, many Christian philosophers have pursued what is now called analytic theology, in which religious doctrines are given formulations which are precise, and it is hoped self-consistent and otherwise defensible. This article surveys these recent "rational reconstructions" of the Trinity doctrine, which employ concepts from contemporary analytic metaphysics, logic, and epistemology.

One of the points where there is a significant, long-lasting intersection of the interests of many philosophers with the interests of many people of all kinds and conditions concerns the nature and significance of death. How should we understand the mortality of all living things and, closer to home, how should we understand our own mortality? Is it possible for persons to survive biological death? This is a topic that has occupied both analytic and continental philosophy in the twentieth century (e.g., Fred Feldman, Martin Heidegger). When the topic of death is ignored or denied in popular culture, some philosophers, theologians, social and political critics rail against the inauthentic complacency of ignoring one of the most important facts about our lives (see, for example, S ren Kierkegaard's essay "At a Graveside" or Ernest Becker's famous 1974 book, The Denial of Death). But what precisely are the facts of death? Is it true that a person is annihilated when she dies, or is there a possibility or even a likelihood that she may survive death? Are any of the religious conceptions of an afterlife promising from a philosophical point of view? There are five sections in this entry. In the first, we propose that beliefs about death and the possibility of an afterlife are of enduring significance because of our care for persons here and now, and thus our concern for their future and our own. Just as it is reasonable to hope that those we love have a fulfilling future in this life, it is natural to consider whether this life is the only life there is and, if there is reason to believe that there is an afterlife (or a life beyond this life), it would be reasonable to hope that this might involve a new, valuable environment or at least one that is not Hellish in nature. We bring to the fore other reasons why the topic of an afterlife is philosophically interesting. In sections two and three we consider the concept and possibility that persons survive death in light of two substantial philosophies of mind: dualism (section two) and materialism (section three). Section four addresses the afterlife in terms of empirical evidence. In section five, reasons are advanced for thinking that the reasonability of beliefs about an afterlife depends on the reasonability of metaphysical convictions.
6.3 Description SEP: The philosophical investigation into the possibility of personal survival after biological death. It intersects with questions about the nature of the soul, identity, and the significance of mortality.

6.2 Description SEP: The purpose of this entry is to explore how atheism and agnosticism are related to theism and, more importantly, to each other. This requires examining the surprisingly contentious issue of how best to define the terms "atheism" and "agnosticism". Settling this issue, at least for the purposes of this entry, will set the stage for discussing an important distinction between global atheism and local atheism, which in turn will be helpful for distinguishing different forms of agnosticism. Examination of an argument in support of a modest form of agnosticism will ensue, followed by discussion of three arguments for atheism and one argument against a more ambitious form of agnosticism.
6.3 Description SEP: The philosophical exploration of the rejection of belief in God (atheism) and the suspension of judgment regarding God's existence (agnosticism). It examines the definitions, logical relationships, and epistemic justifications for these positions.

The evidential problem of evil for theism: whether evil's existence makes God's existence improbable. Arguments cite gratuitous suffering apparently serving no greater good. Responses include skeptical theism, soul-making theodicies, and free will defense. Debates distinguish logical from evidential versions and natural from moral evil.

6.2 Description SEP: A miracle (from the Latin mirari, to wonder), at a first and very rough approximation, is an event that is not explicable by natural causes alone. A reported miracle excites wonder because it appears to require, as its cause, something beyond the reach of human action and natural causes. Historically, the appeal to miracles has formed one of the primary lines of argument in favor of specific forms of theism, the argument typically being that the event in question can best (or can only) be explained as the act of a particular deity.
6.3 Description SEP: Events not explicable by natural causes, attributed to divine intervention. Hume's argument against miracles claims testimony can never outweigh evidence for laws of nature. Debates concern definition (violation of law vs. sign), probability, and the possibility of identifying miracles historically.

6.2 Description SEP: Theists believe that reality's ultimate principle is God-an omnipotent, omniscient, goodness that is the creative ground of everything other than itself. Monotheism is the view that there is only one such God. After a brief discussion of monotheism's historical origins, this entry looks at the five most influential attempts to establish God's uniqueness. We will consider arguments from God's simplicity, from his perfection, from his sovereignty, from his omnipotence, and from his demand for total devotion. The entry concludes by examining three major theistic traditions which contain strands which might seem at odds with their commitment to monotheism-the Jewish Kabbalistic tradition, Christianity, and Shri Vaishnavism.
6.3 Description SEP: Belief in a single, universal, personal God. Topics include divine attributes (omnipotence, omniscience), the Trinity (in Christianity), and coherence of monotheism. Contrasts with polytheism, henotheism, and pantheism. Major religions (Judaism, Christianity, Islam) articulate distinct monotheistic theologies.

6.2 Description SEP: The term 'pantheism' is a modern one, possibly first appearing in the writing of the Irish freethinker John Toland (1705) and constructed from the Greek roots pan (all) and theos (God). But if not the name, the ideas themselves are very ancient, and any survey of the history of philosophy will uncover numerous pantheist or pantheistically inclined thinkers; although it should also be noted that in many cases all that history has preserved for us are second-hand reportings of attributed doctrines, any reconstruction of which is too conjectural to provide much by way of philosophical illumination. At its most general, pantheism may be understood positively as the view that God is identical with the cosmos, the view that there exists nothing which is outside of God, or else negatively as the rejection of any view that considers God as distinct from the universe. However, given the complex and contested nature of the concepts involved, there is insufficient consensus among philosophers to permit the construction of any more detailed definition not open to serious objection from some quarter or other. Moreover, the label is a controversial one, where strong desires either to appropriate or to reject it often serve only to obscure the actual issues, and it would be a sad irony if pantheism revealed itself to be most like a traditional religion in its sectarian disputes over just what counts as 'true pantheism.' Therefore pantheism should not be thought of as a single codifiable position. Rather it should be understood as a diverse family of distinct doctrines; many of whom would be surprised-and, indeed, disconcerted-to find themselves regarded as members of a single household. Further, since the concept has porous and disputed boundaries there is no clear consensus on just who qualifies, no definitive roll-call of past pantheists. Given this situation the range of things that may be usefully said about all pantheisms is perhaps limited, but nonetheless a variety of concepts may be clarified, the nature of contentious issues explored, and the range of possible options more precisely mapped out.
6.3 Description SEP: View that God and the Universe are identical. 'Deus sive Natura' (Spinoza). Rejects a personal, transcendent creator. Associated with Stoicism, Bruno, and Spinoza. Debates concern whether it is distinct from atheism ('polite atheism', Schopenhauer) or a deep religious insight.

6.2 Description SEP: "Panentheism" is a constructed word composed of the English equivalents of the Greek terms "pan", meaning all, "en", meaning in, and "theism", derived from the Greek 'theos' meaning God. Panentheism considers God and the world to be inter-related with the world being in God and God being in the world. While panentheism offers an increasingly popular alternative to classical theism, both panentheism and classical theistic systems affirm divine transcendence and immanence. But, classical theistic systems by prioritizing the difference between God and the world reject any influence by the world upon God while panentheism affirms the world's influence upon God. On the other hand, while pantheism emphasizes God's identity with the world, panentheism maintains the identity and significance of the non-divine. Anticipations of panentheistic understandings of God have occurred in both philosophical and theological writings throughout history (Hartshorne and Reese 1953; J. Cooper, 2006). However, a rich diversity of panentheistic understandings has developed in the past two centuries primarily in Christian traditions responding to scientific thought (Clayton and Peacocke 2004a). Although panentheism generally emphasizes God's presence in the world without losing the distinct identity of either God or the world, specific forms of panentheism, drawing from different sources, explain the nature of the relationship of God to the world in a variety of ways and come to different conclusions about the nature of the significance of the world for the identity of God. Panentheists have responded to two primary criticisms: (1) the panentheistic God is a limited God, and (2) panentheism cannot be distinguished from other forms of theism such as classical theism or pantheism.
6.3 Description SEP: View that the world is 'in' God, but God exceeds the world. 'Everything is in God, God is in everything'. Distinguishes from pantheism (God = World) and classical theism (God is separate). Found in Hegel, Process Theology (Whitehead), and mystical traditions.

3 Topic Group: Probability in the Philosophy of Religion *

6.2 Description SEP: A prophet is a person who plays as special role mediating the relationship between other people and the divine. People typically envision prophecy in terms of God communicating through a prophet to others some important information that could not have been known to the prophet in any ordinary way. Prophecy is interesting from a philosophical point of view for many reasons, including the fact that it raises compelling questions about divine knowledge and communication, human language, the nature of time, and human freedom. Unlike theologians or apologists, philosophers rarely argue about who has actually prophesied what, or whether or not a given prophecy came true. Instead, they prefer to argue about ideal cases, where the theoretical issues can be clearly identified. In this article, we will follow their lead by sidestepping completely the question of whether or not any apparent prophecies have actually been fulfilled. We will also avoid the question of the existence of God, which is addressed by a number of other entries in this encyclopedia (see the entries listed in the Related Entries section below).
6.3 Description SEP: Philosophical issues of receiving divine messages. Maimonides viewed it as intellectual perfection (imagination + intellect). Spinoza reduced it to vivid imagination. Epistemic problem: How to distinguish true prophet from madman? Also concerns theological fatalism (future contingents).

6.2 Description SEP: With respect to many, if not most issues, there exist significant differences of opinion among individuals who seem to be equally knowledgeable and sincere. Individuals who apparently have access to the same information and are equally interested in the truth do in fact affirm incompatible perspectives on, for instance, significant social, political, and economic issues. Such diversity of opinion, though, is nowhere more evident than in the area of religious thought. On almost every religious issue there are honest, knowledgeable people who hold significantly diverse, often incompatible beliefs. Religious diversity of this sort can fruitfully be explored in many ways -for instance, from psychological, anthropological, or historical perspectives. The current discussion, however, will concern itself primarily with those key issues surrounding religious diversity with which philosophers, especially analytic philosophers of religion, are most concerned at present. Specifically, our discussion will focus primarily on the following questions: How pervasive is religious diversity? Does the reality of this diversity require a response? Can a person who acknowledges religious diversity remain justified in claiming just one perspective to be correct? If so, is it morally justifiable to attempt to convert others to a different perspective? Can it justifiably be claimed that only one religion offers a path into the eternal presence of God? How should religious diversity be approached in public education? The answers to such questions are not simply academic; they have practical implications. They increasingly have great impact on how we treat others, both personally and corporately. The main goal of this discussion, it is important to note, is not to support a given perspective on these issues. It is to clarify the issues and explore differing perspectives.

6.3 Description SEP: The fact of diverse, incompatible religious beliefs among apparently equally informed and sincere believers. Philosophical responses include exclusivism (only one religion is true), inclusivism (one religion is truest but others contain partial truth), and pluralism (multiple religions are equally valid paths). Debates concern rationality of religious belief given diversity.

6 Description GPT5: Inquiry into value across domains-ethical, aesthetic, prudential, political-distinguishing intrinsic from instrumental worth and clarifying the normative reasons that guide appraisal.
6.1 Description Gemini: The branch of philosophy that deals with questions of value. This includes ethics (the study of moral value), aesthetics (the study of artistic and natural beauty), and social and political philosophy (the study of the values that structure societies).
6.2 Description SEP: The term "value theory" is used in at least three different ways in philosophy. In its broadest sense, "value theory" is a catch-all label used to encompass all branches of moral philosophy, social and political philosophy, aesthetics, and sometimes feminist philosophy and the philosophy of religion - whatever areas of philosophy are deemed to encompass some "evaluative" aspect. In its narrowest sense, "value theory" is used for a relatively narrow area of normative ethical theory particularly, but not exclusively, of concern to consequentialists. In this narrow sense, "value theory" is roughly synonymous with "axiology". Axiology can be thought of as primarily concerned with classifying what things are good, and how good they are. For instance, a traditional question of axiology concerns whether the objects of value are subjective psychological states, or objective states of the world. But in a more useful sense, "value theory" designates the area of moral philosophy that is concerned with theoretical questions about value and goodness of all varieties - the theory of value. The theory of value, so construed, encompasses axiology, but also includes many other questions about the nature of value and its relation to other moral categories. The division of moral theory into the theory of value, as contrasting with other areas of investigation, cross-cuts the traditional classification of moral theory into normative and metaethical inquiry, but is a worthy distinction in its own right; theoretical questions about value constitute a core domain of interest in moral theory, often cross the boundaries between the normative and the metaethical, and have a distinguished history of investigation. This article surveys a range of the questions which come up in the theory of value, and attempts to impose some structure on the terrain by including some observations about how they are related to one another.

6 Description GPT5: Study of aesthetic value, art, and beauty, centered on aesthetic experience and judgment and on how form and meaning contribute to worth.
6.1 Description Gemini: The subfield of value theory concerned with the nature of art, beauty, and taste. It explores the creation and appreciation of beauty, the principles of artistic judgment, and the nature of aesthetic experience.

6 Description GPT5: How aesthetic practices yield cognitive gains-insight, understanding, concept-formation-beyond pleasure, often via imagination and trained perception.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of the mental processes involved in the appreciation of art and beauty, including perception, emotion, imagination, and judgment.

6 Description GPT5: Accounts of the stance apt for appreciation (e.g., disinterested attention vs practical engagement) and what each explains about evaluation.
6.1 Description Gemini: A particular mode of perception or contemplation directed towards an object's appearance, independent of its practical utility or purpose. The concept of disinterestedness is central to this idea.

6 Description GPT5: Framework concepts-beauty, sublime, expression, genre, form-that structure classification, interpretation, and assessment.
6.1 Description Gemini: The concepts and vocabulary used to describe and evaluate aesthetic qualities. Philosophers often distinguish between thin concepts (like beautiful or ugly) and thick concepts (like elegant, graceful, gaudy, or kitschy), which have both descriptive and evaluative content.

6 Description GPT5: Role of emotions in appreciation, including the paradox of negative emotion, catharsis, and emotions as carriers of understanding.
6.1 Description Gemini: The feelings and emotions that are aroused by artworks or aesthetic objects. This field explores the nature of these emotions and addresses puzzles like the paradox of fiction (how we can be moved by things we know are not real).

6 Description GPT5: The phenomenology of appreciation-absorbed attention, seeing-as, and pattern sensitivity-distinguishing it from ordinary perception.
6.1 Description Gemini: The unique, often pleasurable, and subjectively valuable state of mind that arises from engaging with an object's aesthetic qualities. It is often considered to be an experience valued for its own sake.

6 Description GPT5: Claims to normativity (and sometimes universality) in verdicts such as "beautiful," with reasons competing with mere preference.
6.1 Description Gemini: A judgment concerning the aesthetic value of an object, such as "This painting is beautiful." A central problem, famously analyzed by Kant, is how such judgments can be based on subjective feeling yet claim a kind of universal validity.
6.2 Description SEP: Beauty is an important part of our lives. Ugliness too. It is no surprise then that philosophers since antiquity have been interested in our experiences of and judgments about beauty and ugliness. They have tried to understand the nature of these experiences and judgments, and they have also wanted to know whether these experiences and judgments were legitimate. Both these projects took a sharpened form in the twentieth century, when this part of our lives came under a sustained attack in both European and North American intellectual circles. Much of the discourse about beauty since the eighteenth century had deployed a notion of the "aesthetic", and so that notion in particular came in for criticism. This disdain for the aesthetic may have roots in a broader cultural Puritanism, which fears the connection between the aesthetic and pleasure. At one time, from the 1960s to the 1990s, even to suggest that an artwork might be good because it is pleasurable, as opposed to cognitively, morally or politically beneficial, was to court derision. (This is less true now.) The twentieth century was not kind to the notions of beauty or the aesthetic. Nevertheless, there were always some thinkers-philosophers, as well as others in the study of particular arts-who persisted in thinking seriously about beauty and the aesthetic. In the first part of this essay, we will look at the particularly rich account of judgments of beauty given to us by Immanuel Kant. The notion of a "judgment of taste" is central to Kant's account and also to virtually everyone working in traditional aesthetics; so we begin by examining Kant's characterization of the judgment of taste. In the second part, we look at the issues that twentieth century thinkers raised. In the third part, we consider disinterestedness, which is taken by Kant to be part of the judgment of taste. We end, the fourth part, by drawing on Kant's account of the judgment of taste to consider whether the notion of the aesthetic is viable.
6.3 Description SEP: The examination of judgments about beauty and artistic merit. Key questions involve the universality, subjectivity, and normative force of such evaluations.

6 Description GPT5: How perception is calibrated by form and style; expertise refines aspect perception (e.g., seeing-in) and what properties are noticed.
6.1 Description Gemini: The idea that appreciating aesthetic qualities involves a distinct way of perceiving the world. It is not just seeing an object's physical properties, but seeing it as, for example, balanced, dynamic, or somber.

6 Description GPT5: Whether pleasure is constitutive, merely evidential, or contingent upon value; distinction between aesthetic and sensory pleasure.
6.1 Description Gemini: A special kind of enjoyment or satisfaction derived from aesthetic experience. It is often distinguished from mere sensory gratification by its connection to our cognitive faculties and its disinterested nature.

6 Description GPT5: Standards of taste as capacities for discriminating reasons-responsive pleasure; the true judge ideal and the possibility of improvement.
6.1 Description Gemini: The capacity to perceive and judge aesthetic qualities accurately and with discernment. Debates center on whether taste is purely subjective or if it can be cultivated and standardized.

6 Description GPT5: A cognitive achievement: grasp of structure, style, and significance that explains why a work merits its evaluation.
6.1 Description Gemini: A cognitive state of grasping the meaning, form, and significance of an artwork or aesthetic object, which goes deeper than mere judgment or preference.

6 Description GPT5: Links to cognitive science (attention, prediction, expertise) and neuroscience (reward, emotion), modeling mechanisms of response.
6.1 Description Gemini: The interdisciplinary field that uses methods from cognitive science, psychology, and neuroscience to investigate the mental and neural underpinnings of aesthetic experience, judgment, and creation.

6 Description GPT5: Properties such as grace, elegance, unity, expressiveness; debates over response-dependence and explanatory role.
6.1 Description Gemini: The properties of an object, such as its form, color, or composition, that are relevant to its aesthetic value.

6 Description GPT5: Accounts of beauty-as unity-in-variety, fittingness, or expressive form-and its relation to moral or cognitive value.
6.1 Description Gemini: A core aesthetic concept referring to a quality that arouses aesthetic pleasure and admiration. Traditionally defined by principles like harmony, proportion, symmetry, and clarity.
6.2 Description SEP: The nature of beauty is one of the most enduring and controversial themes in Western philosophy, and is-with the nature of art-one of the two fundamental issues in philosophical aesthetics. Beauty has traditionally been counted among the ultimate values, with goodness, truth, and justice. It is a primary theme among ancient Greek, Hellenistic, and medieval philosophers, and was central to eighteenth and nineteenth-century thought, as represented in treatments by such thinkers as Shaftesbury, Hutcheson, Hume, Burke, Kant, Schiller, Hegel, Schopenhauer, Hanslick, and Santayana. By the beginning of the twentieth century, beauty was in decline as a subject of philosophical inquiry, and also as a primary goal of the arts. However, there were signs of revived interest by the early 2000s. This article will begin with a sketch of the debate over whether beauty is objective or subjective, which is perhaps the single most-prosecuted disagreement in the literature. It will proceed to set out some of the major approaches to or theories of beauty developed within Western philosophical and artistic traditions.
6.3 Description SEP: One of the fundamental values and central problems in aesthetics, addressed since ancient Greek philosophy. Debates concern whether beauty is objective or subjective, whether it resides in objects or experiences, and its relationship to pleasure and taste. Historical treatments range from Plato's Form of Beauty to eighteenth-century theories of aesthetic judgment.

6 Description GPT5: Style as a patterned set of choices-technique, form, and expression-used for identification, interpretation, and appraisal.
6.1 Description Gemini: The distinctive manner of creating or presenting an artwork that is characteristic of a particular artist, movement, period, or culture.

6 Description GPT5: Families of views about the metaphysics of aesthetic truth/value: realism, subjectivism, relativism, and universalism.
6.1 Description Gemini: The meta-aesthetic debate over the objective status of aesthetic properties and judgments.

6 Description GPT5: Realism: some aesthetic properties are mind-independent and judgments about them are truth-apt and objective.
6.1 Description Gemini: The view that aesthetic properties (like beauty) are real, objective features of the world that exist independently of the perceptions and responses of any observer.

6 Description GPT5: Relativism: aesthetic truth is indexed to frameworks (cultures, historical periods, standards), shaping disagreement and appraisal.
6.1 Description Gemini: The view that the truth or justification of aesthetic judgments is not absolute, but is relative to a particular culture, historical period, or conceptual framework.
6.2 Description SEP: Relativism, roughly put, is the view that truth and falsity, right and wrong, standards of reasoning, and procedures of justification are products of differing conventions and frameworks of assessment and that their authority is confined to the context giving rise to them. More precisely, "relativism" covers views which maintain that-at a high level of abstraction-at least some class of things have the properties they have (e.g., beautiful, morally good, epistemically justified) not simpliciter, but only relative to a given framework of assessment (e.g., local cultural norms, individual standards), and correspondingly, that the truth of claims attributing these properties holds only once the relevant framework of assessment is specified or supplied. Relativists characteristically insist, furthermore, that if something is only relatively so, then there can be no framework-independent vantage point from which the matter of whether the thing in question is so can be established. Relativism has been, in its various guises, both one of the most popular and most reviled philosophical doctrines of our time. Defenders see it as a harbinger of tolerance and the only ethical and epistemic stance worthy of the open-minded and tolerant. Detractors dismiss it for its alleged incoherence and uncritical intellectual permissiveness. Debates about relativism permeate the whole spectrum of philosophical sub-disciplines. From ethics to epistemology, science to religion, political theory to ontology, theories of meaning and even logic, philosophy has felt the need to respond to this heady and seemingly subversive idea. Discussions of relativism often also invoke considerations relevant to the very nature and methodology of philosophy and to the division between the so-called "analytic and continental" camps in philosophy. And yet, despite a long history of debate going back to Plato and an increasingly large body of writing, it is still difficult to come to an agreed definition of what, at its core, relativism is, and what philosophical import it has. This entry attempts to provide a broad account of the many ways in which "relativism" has been defined, explained, defended and criticized.

6 Description GPT5: Subjectivism: value rests primarily in individual responses (pleasure, preference), often via response-dependent accounts.
6.1 Description Gemini: The view that aesthetic judgments are simply expressions of an individual's personal preferences or emotional responses. On this view, "This is beautiful" means nothing more than "I like this."

6 Description GPT5: Universality: aesthetic judgments present a claim on others (a "universal voice"), grounded in shared faculties or public reasons.
6.1 Description Gemini: The idea that some aesthetic judgments or values are valid for all human beings, regardless of their individual or cultural background.

6 Description GPT5: How artworks carry meaning-mimesis, symbolism, expression, narrative-and how form interfaces with content.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of how artworks and aesthetic objects represent the world and convey meaning.

6 Description GPT5: Rule-governed symbol systems (notations, iconography) whose conventions guide creation and interpretation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The theory, most associated with Nelson Goodman, that artworks function as symbols within complex systems, much like languages. It analyzes how different art forms (e.g., painting, music, literature) use distinct symbolic rules to refer to and structure our experience.

6 Description GPT5: Theories of depiction-resemblance, structural mapping, and seeing-in-and their constraints on content reference.
6.1 Description Gemini: The way a two-dimensional surface (like a painting) represents a three-dimensional object or scene. Theories debate whether depiction is based on resemblance, convention, or the kind of experience it elicits in the viewer.
6.2 Description SEP: Depiction or pictorial representation was studied less intensively by philosophers than linguistic meaning until the 1960s. The traditional doctrine that pictures represent objects by copying their appearance had been challenged by art theorists since the first quarter of the twentieth century, when what were thought of as illusionistic styles of painting lost favour, due to the growing prestige of so-called "primitive" artistic styles, and the fauvist and cubist experiments of artists at that time. But it took several decades before philosophers became interested in these debates. When they did so, it was largely due to the impact of two books: Ernst Gombrich's Art and Illusion (1960), and Nelson Goodman's Languages of Art (1968). Gombrich explored a variety of problems about the nature of depiction, the evolution of style, and the nature of realism, drawing extensively on theories of visual perception advanced since Helmholtz, and on Karl Popper's falsificationist theory of science. Goodman, by contrast defended a purely conventionalist theory of depiction in general and realistic depiction in particular, which was rooted in the nominalist philosophy he had developed in collaboration with W.V.O. Quine. A large part of the philosophical work on this topic during the past fifty years consists in attempts to develop theories of depiction in general, and realistic depiction in particular, which overcome the objections to their views, while avoiding the simplifications and alleged cultural bias of the traditional ideas they rejected.
6.3 Description SEP: Pictorial representation, where images represent objects or scenes. Debates concern whether depiction involves resemblance, information content, or recognitional capacities. Challenges to resemblance theories from abstract and unrealistic art led to alternative accounts based on seeing-in or symbol systems.

6 Description GPT5: Debates over intentionalism versus anti-intentionalism: whether authorial intention fixes meaning or audience/context can override it.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical debate concerning the role of the artist's intention in determining the correct interpretation and meaning of an artwork.

6 Description GPT5: Taxonomy of aesthetic value and its relation to moral and epistemic value; intrinsic vs instrumental contributions.
6.1 Description Gemini: The value an object, event, or state of affairs possesses in virtue of its aesthetic features.

6 Description GPT5: Practice of reason-giving criticism aiming at interpretive insight, comparative evaluation, and audience guidance.
6.1 Description Gemini: The practice of describing, interpreting, and evaluating artworks, often with the goal of enhancing the audience's understanding and appreciation.

6 Description GPT5: Standards and criteria (e.g., unity, complexity, intensity, originality) balancing reasons with appropriately trained response.
6.1 Description Gemini: The act of forming a judgment about the artistic merit or aesthetic success of an artwork.

6 Description GPT5: The oughts of appreciation-requirements of attention, sensitivity, and consistency-that make judgments accountable to reasons.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of the normative force of aesthetic judgments. It asks whether there are standards for taste and whether some aesthetic judgments are better than others. If a painting is beautiful, does that give others a reason to find it beautiful?

6 Description GPT5: Intersections of aesthetic and ethical value: ethicism, autonomism, moralism, and whether ethical flaws can be aesthetic flaws.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical exploration of the relationship between aesthetics and morality. It addresses questions like whether an artwork's moral character affects its aesthetic value (moralism vs. autonomism).

6 Description GPT5: How culture, institutions, and power shape standards, genres, and canons; issues of identity, appropriation, and inclusion.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of how cultural factors and social contexts shape the creation, interpretation, and evaluation of art.

6 Description GPT5: Candidates for cross-cultural universals (e.g., symmetry, rhythm) and how they coexist with enculturation and diversity.
6.1 Description Gemini: Qualities, principles, or preferences in art and aesthetics that are considered to be common across all human cultures and historical periods.

6 Description GPT5: Comparative analysis across traditions that builds non-ethnocentric frameworks and translates between differing conceptual schemes.
6.1 Description Gemini: The comparative study of aesthetic theories, artistic traditions, and values from different cultures, challenging a purely Western-centric view of aesthetics.

6 Description GPT5: Philosophy of popular art and mass media-authenticity, kitsch, production/consumption-and their social value.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical analysis of popular art forms like film, television, rock music, and comics. It often questions the traditional distinction between high art and low art.

6 Description GPT5: Appreciation of nature-landscapes, species, weather-via scientific, imaginative, or environmental models, with ties to conservation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The branch of aesthetics concerned with the appreciation of natural environments and objects (e.g., landscapes, sunsets, animals), as distinct from human-made art.

6 Description GPT5: The ontology and value of artworks: type-token identity, performance and reproduction, authorship, and material vs digital media.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical inquiry into the fundamental nature of art and artworks.

6 Description GPT5: What makes something an artwork-artifactuality, maker intentions, historical/contextual placement, and uptake within practices.
6.1 Description Gemini: The objects or events that are the products of artistic practice. Key philosophical questions concern their ontological status (e.g., is a musical work the score, the performance, or an abstract object?).

6 Description GPT5: The artworld as a network of roles and institutions (artists, curators, critics, audiences) that constitute and legitimate art practices.
6.1 Description Gemini: A term coined by philosopher Arthur Danto to describe the institutional and historical context-including artists, critics, galleries, and theoretical discourse-that is necessary for an object to be considered a work of art.

6 Description GPT5: Competing definitions of art-representational, expressivist, formalist, institutional, historical, and cluster accounts-assessing necessity and sufficiency.
6.1 Description Gemini: The long-standing philosophical challenge of providing a set of necessary and sufficient conditions to distinguish art from non-art.
6.2 Description SEP: The definition of art is controversial in contemporary philosophy. Whether art can be defined has also been a matter of controversy. The philosophical usefulness of a definition of art has also been debated. Contemporary definitions can be classified with respect to the dimensions of art they emphasize. One distinctively modern, conventionalist, sort of definition focuses on art's institutional features, emphasizing the way art changes over time, modern works that appear to break radically with all traditional art, the relational properties of artworks that depend on works' relations to art history, art genres, etc. - more broadly, on the undeniable heterogeneity of the class of artworks. The more traditional, less conventionalist sort of definition defended in contemporary philosophy makes use of a broader, more traditional concept of aesthetic properties that includes more than art-relational ones, and puts more emphasis on art's pan-cultural and trans-historical characteristics - in sum, on commonalities across the class of artworks. Hybrid definitions aim to do justice to both the traditional aesthetic dimension as well as to the institutional and art-historical dimensions of art, while privileging neither.

6 Description GPT5: Sources of art's value-aesthetic, cognitive, moral, social, therapeutic-and whether value is intrinsic or instrumental to human flourishing.
6.1 Description Gemini: The investigation into why art is valuable to individuals and society. This includes its capacity to provide pleasure, express emotion, impart knowledge, and foster moral or social understanding.

6 Description GPT5: Practical inquiry that applies ethical theories to concrete cases, policies, and institutions, translating general norms into action-guidance via tools like principlism, casuistry, and risk-benefit analysis.
6.1 Description Gemini: The branch of ethics that involves the practical application of moral norms and principles to specific, controversial issues and professions. It seeks to resolve complex moral dilemmas in real-world contexts.

6 Description GPT5: Cross-cutting questions about how to justify norms in practice-balancing consequences, rights, and virtues; handling trade-offs, uncertainty, and moral residue.

6 Description GPT5: Wrong-making features that set back interests or violate rights; includes nonmaleficence, risk, and double-effect considerations.

6 Description GPT5: A commitment to truthfulness, non-deception, and promise-keeping, constrained by privacy and justified confidentiality.
6.1 Description Gemini: The moral duty to be truthful in communication and to act with sincerity and integrity.

6 Description GPT5: Fair distribution and rectification of goods and burdens; contrasts egalitarian, prioritarian, sufficientarian, and desert-based models.
6.1 Description Gemini: The principle of fairness in the distribution of resources, opportunities, benefits, and burdens within a society. Key theories include distributive justice, retributive justice, and restorative justice.
6.2 Description SEP: The idea of justice occupies centre stage both in ethics, and in legal and political philosophy. We apply it to individual actions, to laws, and to public policies, and we think in each case that if they are unjust this is a strong, maybe even conclusive, reason to reject them. Classically, justice was counted as one of the four cardinal virtues (and sometimes as the most important of the four); in modern times John Rawls famously described it as 'the first virtue of social institutions' (Rawls 1971, p.3; Rawls, 1999, p.3). We might debate which of these realms of practical philosophy has first claim on justice: is it first and foremost a property of the law, for example, and only derivatively a property of individuals and other institutions? But it is probably more enlightening to accept that the idea has over time sunk deep roots in each of these domains, and to try to make sense of such a wide-ranging concept by identifying elements that are present whenever justice is invoked, but also examining the different forms it takes in various practical contexts. This article aims to provide a general map of the ways in which justice has been understood by philosophers, past and present. We begin by identifying four core features that distinguish justice from other moral and political ideas. We then examine some major conceptual contrasts: between conservative and ideal justice, between corrective and distributive justice, between procedural and substantive justice, and between comparative and non-comparative justice. Next we turn to questions of scope: to who or what do principles of justice apply? We ask whether non-human animals can be subjects of justice, whether justice applies only between people who already stand in a particular kind of relationship to one another, and whether individual people continue to have duties of justice once justice-based institutions have been created. We then examine three overarching theories that might serve to unify the different forms of justice: utilitarianism, contractarianism, and egalitarianism. But it seems, in conclusion, that no such theory is likely be successful. More detailed discussions of particular forms of justice can be found in other entries: see especially distributive justice, global justice, intergenerational justice, international distributive justice, justice and bad luck, justice as a virtue, and retributive justice.
6.3 Description SEP: Moral and political concept concerning fairness, rights, and what is owed. Distributive justice addresses resource allocation; retributive justice concerns punishment; procedural justice involves fair processes. Major theories include utilitarianism, Rawls's fairness as justice, libertarianism, and communitarianism. Justice is called the first virtue of institutions.

6 Description GPT5: Obligation to obey legitimate law vs duties to civil disobedience when legal norms conflict with justice or rights.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical obligation to obey the laws of a state or governing body. This field explores the moral grounds for this obligation and the conditions under which civil disobedience might be justified.

6 Description GPT5: Interference for another's good without consent; evaluated against autonomy, harm prevention, and standards for soft vs hard paternalism.
6.1 Description Gemini: The act of interfering with a person's autonomy or liberty, against their will, but justified by a claim that the action is for their own good. The debate centers on the conflict between the principles of autonomy and beneficence.
6.2 Description SEP: Paternalism is the interference of a state or an individual with another person, against their will, and defended or motivated by a claim that the person interfered with will be better off or protected from harm. The issue of paternalism arises with respect to restrictions by the law such as anti-drug legislation, the compulsory wearing of seatbelts, and in medical contexts by the withholding of relevant information concerning a patient's condition by physicians. At the theoretical level it raises questions of how persons should be treated when they are less than fully rational.
6.3 Description SEP: Interference with a person's liberty for their own good (e.g., seatbelt laws). Hard paternalism coerces adults; soft paternalism protects the incompetent. Mill's Harm Principle rejects it. Libertarian Paternalism (Nudge) uses choice architecture to guide decisions without coercion.

6 Description GPT5: Entitlements that generate claims and duties (negative vs positive); frameworks include human rights and capabilities approaches.
6.1 Description Gemini: Justified claims or entitlements that individuals and groups have upon others or upon society. Rights can be positive (an entitlement to something, like healthcare) or negative (an entitlement to be free from something, like interference).
6.2 Description SEP: Rights are entitlements (not) to perform certain actions, or (not) to be in certain states; or entitlements that others (not) perform certain actions or (not) be in certain states. Rights dominate modern understandings of what actions are permissible and which institutions are just. Rights structure the form of governments, the content of laws, and the shape of morality as many now see it. To accept a set of rights is to approve a distribution of freedom and authority, and so to endorse a certain view of what may, must, and must not be done. This entry begins by describing the nature of rights: their classification, their composition, and their function. It then reviews the history of the language of rights, and various relationships between rights and reasons. The major contemporary philosophical approaches to the justification of rights are compared, and the entry concludes by surveying criticisms of rights and "rights talk." The focus throughout is on general theoretical issues (what rights are) and not on arguments over specific rights (what rights there are). Those looking for fuller introductions to rights may consider Jones (1994), Harel (2005), Campbell (2006), Ivison (2007) and Edmundson (2012).
6.3 Description SEP: Entitlements to perform or not be subjected to actions. Hohfeld's analysis: Privilege, Claim, Power, Immunity. Natural vs. Legal rights. Interest Theory (Raz) vs. Will Theory (Hart). Negative rights (liberty) vs. Positive rights (welfare).

6 Description GPT5: Norms governing care at life's end, including goals of care, withholding/withdrawing treatment, and respecting patient values.

6 Description GPT5: Assistance in ending life at a competent person's request; distinguished from euthanasia by who performs the final act.

6 Description GPT5: Criteria for irreversible loss of whole-brain (or brain-stem) function; guides organ procurement and end-of-life decisions.

6 Description GPT5: Philosophical and clinical accounts demarcating death from severe disability or coma; whole-brain, higher-brain, and cardiorespiratory standards.

6 Description GPT5: Intentionally ending a life to relieve suffering; debates voluntary vs non-voluntary, acts vs omissions, and slippery-slope risks.
6.1 Description Gemini: The act of intentionally ending a life to relieve pain and suffering. Key distinctions include active (taking direct action to cause death) vs. passive (withholding treatment) and voluntary vs. involuntary.

6 Description GPT5: Intentional mass killing of protected groups; focuses on prevention, humanitarian intervention, and duties of remembrance and reparation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The systematic and deliberate extermination of a national, racial, political, or cultural group, considered a supreme crime against humanity.

6 Description GPT5: Killing newborns or late-term fetuses; examines moral status, personhood, viability, and parental/social responsibilities.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical examination of the intentional killing of infants.

6 Description GPT5: Technologies and judgments that sustain vital functions; criteria for medical futility, proportionality, and patient/ surrogate authority.

6 Description GPT5: Self-killing and its permissibility, rationality, and preventive obligations; tensions between autonomy and harm to others.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical and ethical arguments concerning the morality of intentionally taking one's own life.
6.2 Description SEP: Throughout history, suicide has evoked an astonishingly wide range of reactions-bafflement, dismissal, heroic glorification, sympathy, anger, moral or religious condemnation-but it is never uncontroversial. For example, suicide has long been a central concern within many academic disciplines, including sociology, anthropology, psychology, and psychiatry. These disciplines converge in the multidisciplinary field of suicidology. But there remain many controversies within suicidology, including which methodologies are best suited to identify the causes of suicide and whether the study of suicide can be fully disentangled from researchers' political or institutional commitments. (Maung 2020; for concerns about the political dimensions of suicide research, see work within 'critical suicidology' such as White et al. 2016) But perhaps the most enduring controversies surrounding suicide are philosophical. For philosophers, suicide raises a host of conceptual, moral, and psychological questions. Among these questions are: What makes a person's behavior suicidal? What motivates such behavior? Is suicide morally permissible, or even morally required in some extraordinary circumstances? Is suicidal behavior rational? Is aiding others to commit suicide morally permissible or even required in some extraordinary circumstances? This article will examine the main currents of historical and contemporary Western philosophical thought surrounding these questions.
6.3 Description SEP: Ethics of self-killing. Forbidden by Aquinas (violates charity) and Kant (using oneself as means). Permitted by Stoics (the 'open door') and Hume (not a crime against God). Modern debate: Rational suicide, Physician-Assisted Suicide, and duty to prevent.

6 Description GPT5: Whether death (and for whom) is bad, via deprivation and comparativist accounts; implications for priority setting and end-of-life care.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical inquiry into why death is considered a bad thing for the person who dies. Theories include the deprivation account, which argues that death is bad because it deprives a person of the future goods of life.

6 Description GPT5: Ethics of use, regulation, and treatment related to psychoactive substances across medical and recreational contexts.

6 Description GPT5: Development and distribution of medicines; trial ethics, access, pricing, and intellectual property tensions.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical issues in the pharmaceutical industry, including the ethics of clinical trials, drug pricing, accessibility, and marketing.

6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical arguments for and against the use, prohibition, and legalization of drugs for non-medical, recreational purposes.

6 Description GPT5: Moral assessment of heritable and somatic interventions; justice in access, safety, and identity concerns.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of ethical, legal, and social issues arising from advances in genetics and biotechnology.

6 Description GPT5: Program to improve populations by controlling reproduction; historical abuses vs modern screening and liberal eugenics debates.
6.1 Description Gemini: The moral and social philosophy of improving the genetic quality of the human population, now widely condemned for its historical association with coercive and discriminatory practices.
6.2 Description SEP: "Eugenics" is a term loaded with historical significance and a strong negative valence. Its literal meaning-good birth-suggests a suitable goal for all prospective parents, yet its historical connotations tie it to the selective breeding programs, horrifying concentration camps, medical experiments, and mass exterminations promoted by Germany's Nazi regime in World War II. Undoubtedly, we have an obligation never to forget the Holocaust, or to allow history to repeat itself. Yet intuitively we have some moral obligation to promote good births-to have, in the most literal sense, eugenic aims. Indeed, if parents are encouraged to provide the best environment for their children (good nutrition, education, health care, a loving family situation, etc.), why not also encourage them to ensure their children have good genes? If we have some moral obligation to secure the well-being of our future children (a question explored extensively in the literature on the non-identity problem; see the entry on the nonidentity problem), different questions come into focus: how far do such obligations extend, what justifies them, and can related contemporary practices be distinguished, in their aims, forms, justifications, and likely consequences, from the clearly morally impermissible eugenic programs of the past? Philosophers have recently begun to explore the possibility of "liberal" as opposed to "authoritative" eugenics (Agar 2004). Liberal eugenics would be based upon individual free choice, pluralist values, and up-to-date scientific understanding of genetics and epigenetics. Furthermore, advocates of liberal eugenics aim to be sensitive to the effects of problematic but deeply entrenched social problems (e.g., racism, sexism, heterosexism) on individual choice. Authoritative eugenics programs, in contrast, were coercive state programs designed to promote social goods, and were based on problematic assumptions about hereditability. Liberal eugenicists point to significant developments in our understanding of genetics to help distinguish contemporary liberal eugenics from its problematic predecessors. Indeed, scientific advances of the last several decades-years that include the advent of in vitro fertilization, the funding and completion of the Human Genome Project, creation of the National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI), and expansions of pre-implantation screening and prenatal testing panels-provide not only more precise understandings of genes and their role in shaping phenotypes and gene-environment interactions, but also a multitude of possibilities for intervention in the process of reproduction. How ought we to use this new knowledge and capacity? This entry offers a short history of the eugenics movement, a survey of arguments that attempt to define and defend liberal eugenics, and coverage of a variety of critiques of those arguments.
6.3 Description SEP: Policies and practices aimed at improving the genetic composition of populations. Historical eugenics movements in the early 20th century led to forced sterilizations and Nazi atrocities. Contemporary debates address genetic screening, selection, and enhancement, raising questions about coercion, discrimination, disability rights, and human dignity versus reproductive autonomy.

6 Description GPT5: Directed modification of genomes for therapy or enhancement; evaluates safety, consent, and equity.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethics of the direct manipulation of an organism's genes using biotechnology. This includes debates over human genetic enhancement and the creation of "designer babies."

6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical issues surrounding the use of genetically modified organisms (GMOs), particularly in agriculture, concerning food safety, environmental impact, and corporate control.

6 Description GPT5: Testing for traits, risks, or parentage; weighs autonomy, privacy, discrimination, and duty to warn relatives.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical dilemmas associated with testing for genetic disorders, including issues of informed consent, privacy of genetic information, and the potential for genetic discrimination.

6 Description GPT5: Ethics of care delivery, financing, and systems design; weighs efficiency against equity and professional duties.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of ethical issues related to the fair provision and distribution of healthcare services.

6 Description GPT5: Justice and accommodation for persons with impairments; rejects ableist norms, emphasizes capabilities and inclusion.

6.3 Description SEP: Philosophical examination of impairment, disability, and society's responses. Questions concern disability's nature (medical versus social model), disabled people's moral status and rights, justice for disabled people, and disability's effects on well-being. The field addresses ableism, accommodation, and identity.

6 Description GPT5: Duties in responding to pathogens, epidemics, and non-communicable diseases; prevention vs treatment priorities.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical and ethical questions about how to define disease, distinguishing it from normal human variation, and the implications for medical treatment.

6 Description GPT5: Value of well-being (health as functioning or capability) and its relation to happiness, preference, and flourishing.
6.1 Description Gemini: The philosophical inquiry into the definition of health and its status as a fundamental human good.

6 Description GPT5: Fair allocation of scarce health resources (e.g., organs, ICU beds) using need, benefit, lottery, or priority to the worse off.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of how to fairly distribute scarce medical resources and ensure equitable access to healthcare for all members of a society.

6 Description GPT5: Entitlements to access, minimum standards, and non-discrimination in care; national vs human-rights frameworks.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical debate over whether access to a certain standard of healthcare is a fundamental human right.

6 Description GPT5: Lived experience of malady, suffering, and meaning-making; complements disease-models with narrative and agency.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical dimensions of the subjective experience of being unwell and the moral duties of caregivers and society toward the ill.

6 Description GPT5: Professional virtues, boundaries, and advocacy in the practice of care; the ethics of dependency and trust.
6.1 Description Gemini: The specific ethical codes, dilemmas, and responsibilities faced by nurses, often focusing on their role as patient advocates.

6 Description GPT5: Policies that secure basic goods (income, housing, care) as preconditions for health; trade-offs in targeting vs universality.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical obligation of the state to ensure the health, safety, and well-being of its citizens.

6 Description GPT5: Norms for clinical practice and research grounded in autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, and justice.
6.1 Description Gemini: The professional code of conduct for physicians and other healthcare providers, traditionally grounded in principles like beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, and justice.

6 Description GPT5: Prospective statements of values and preferences (living wills, proxies) guiding care when capacity is lost.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical and legal status of documents like "living wills" or a "durable power of attorney for healthcare," which allow individuals to specify their wishes for future medical treatment.
6.3 Description SEP: Legal and ethical instruments enabling individuals to specify future medical treatment preferences. They are designed to protect patient autonomy during periods of incapacity.

6 Description GPT5: The duty to promote patients' good while integrating evidence, values, and risks.
6.1 Description Gemini: The core ethical duty of a physician to always act in the best interest of the patient.

6 Description GPT5: Obligations to protect private information and control disclosure, limited by harm and public interest exceptions.
6.1 Description Gemini: The strict ethical and legal duty of healthcare professionals to protect the private medical information of their patients.

6 Description GPT5: Authorization grounded in disclosure, comprehension, and voluntariness; thresholds for capacity and assent.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical principle that requires patients to be given adequate information to make a voluntary and informed decision about their medical care.
6.2 Description SEP: Informed consent is currently treated as the core of bioethics. In clinical practice, the doctrine of informed consent rose to dominance during the course of the 20th century. It replaced a medical ethos founded on trust in physicians' decisions, often on the assumption that "doctor knows best", with an ethos that sought to put patients in charge of their own care. In medical research, the influential Nuremberg Code responded to the cruelty of Nazi experiments stipulating: "The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential". But why should we require informed consent, e.g. when it comes at a cost to the individual's health? What is the content, the scope, and the status of that requirement? How does informed consent in bioethics, the focus of the present entry, relate to consent in sexual ethics, business ethics, and political philosophy?
6.3 Description SEP: The ethical and legal requirement that patients and research subjects voluntarily agree to treatment or participation after receiving adequate information. It replaced paternalistic medical ethics with patient autonomy as the governing principle. Core elements include disclosure, comprehension, voluntariness, competence, and consent itself.

6 Description GPT5: Efforts to extend healthy lifespan; weighs fairness, identity, and priority setting against hoped-for benefits.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical implications of using medical technology to slow or reverse the aging process and dramatically increase the human lifespan.

6 Description GPT5: Standards of competence, negligence, and liability; systems for patient safety and just culture.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical and legal concept of professional negligence by a healthcare provider, where the treatment provided falls below the accepted standard of practice.

6 Description GPT5: Ethical conduct of experimentation: equipoise, risk minimization, independent review, and subject protection.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical principles governing research involving human subjects, including the requirements for informed consent, minimizing risk, and review by an Institutional Review Board (IRB).

6 Description GPT5: Donating organs/tissues under consent (opt-in vs opt-out), altruism, and incentives; combats exploitation.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical issues surrounding the procurement, allocation, and consent process for donating organs for transplantation.
6.3 Description SEP: Ethical issues in procuring and allocating organs for transplantation. Questions concern consent requirements, presumed consent versus opt-in systems, financial incentives, allocation criteria, and defining death. Debates address respecting donors' autonomy while addressing organ shortages and ensuring fair allocation.

6 Description GPT5: Allocation, compatibility, and equity in transplantation; follow-up duties and global organ trade concerns.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical dilemmas in the allocation of scarce transplanted organs, the use of living donors, and the definition of death for organ procurement.

6 Description GPT5: Creating embryos to derive stem cells; weighs potential benefits against embryo moral status and alternatives.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethics of creating cloned human embryos for the purpose of harvesting stem cells for medical research and treatment.

6.2 Description SEP: Neuroethics is an interdisciplinary field focusing on ethical issues raised by our increased and constantly improving understanding of the brain and our ability to monitor and influence it.
6.3 Description SEP: Ethics of neuroscience and neuroscience of ethics. Applied issues: cognitive enhancement, brain reading, responsibility in organic brain disease. Theoretical issues: how neuroscience affects understanding of free will, self, and moral judgment (e.g., debunking moral intuitions).

6 Description GPT5: Use of imaging to infer mind or lie detection; issues of incidental findings, consent, and forensic use.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical implications of using technologies like fMRI and EEG to "read" mental states, including issues of privacy, lie detection, and the diagnosis of mental illness.

6 Description GPT5: Care and decision-making for patients lacking consciousness; standards of best interests and substituted judgment.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical challenges in the diagnosis, treatment, and end-of-life decision-making for patients with severe brain injuries and disorders of consciousness.

6 Description GPT5: Ethics of reproduction, family formation, and repro-technology, balancing autonomy, welfare, and justice.
6.1 Description Gemini: The study of ethical issues surrounding the creation and sustenance of human life, including contraception, abortion, and assisted reproductive technologies.

6 Description GPT5: Moral status of fetal life vs pregnant person's rights; exceptions, late-term issues, and public policy.
6.1 Description Gemini: The intense moral debate over the moral status of the fetus and the permissibility of terminating a pregnancy.

6 Description GPT5: Creating genetically identical organisms or persons; identity, consent, and societal effects.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical arguments for and against the creation of a genetically identical copy of an organism, particularly a human being.
6.2 Description SEP: Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from a somatic (body) cell, came into the world innocent as a lamb. However, soon after the announcement of her birth in February 1997 (Wilmut et al., 1997) she caused panic and controversy. An important, and for many people troubling question arose: if the cloning of sheep is possible, will scientists soon start cloning humans as well; and if they did, would this be wrong or unwise? For most people, Dolly was really a wolf in sheep's clothing. She represented a first undesirable and dangerous step to applying reproductive cloning in humans, something that many agreed should never be done. Only a small minority thought it was permissible, or even morally obligatory to conduct further research into human reproductive cloning. Some had no strong objections to it, but did not see any reason to promote it either. Dolly is now stuffed and set up for display in the National Museum of Scotland. Many countries or jurisdictions have legally banned human cloning or are in the process of doing so. In some countries, including France and Singapore, reproductive cloning of humans is a criminal offence. In 2005, UNESCO adopted a 'Declaration on Human Cloning', which calls for a universal ban on human cloning (for an examination of the human cloning debate at UNESCO since 2008, see Langlois, 2017). The debate on human reproductive cloning seems to have drawn to a close. However, since reproductive cloning of mammals has become routine in several countries, there is reason to believe that at some point in the future, humans will be cloned too. Moreover, even if reproductive cloning will not be possible in the near future, cloning for research and therapeutic purposes is likely to be. This entry describes the most important areas of disagreement regarding the ethics of cloning. I will focus on human cloning (as opposed to animal cloning), since human cloning has been the focus of the cloning debate.
6.3 Description SEP: Creating genetically identical organisms, made famous by Dolly the sheep in 1997. Reproductive cloning aims to create viable organisms, while therapeutic cloning produces embryos for stem cells. Ethical debates concern human dignity, harm to clones, playing God, and whether reproductive or therapeutic cloning should be permitted.

6 Description GPT5: Control of fertility for medical and personal reasons; access, adolescents, and conscience clauses.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical considerations surrounding the use of methods or devices to prevent pregnancy.

6 Description GPT5: Whether and when bringing persons into existence is permissible or obligatory; population ethics and climate implications.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethical questions surrounding the decision of whether to have children, including concerns about overpopulation and the welfare of the potential child.

6 Description GPT5: Choosing offspring traits or sex; discrimination, family balance, and social externalities.
6.1 Description Gemini: The ethics of using medical technology to choose the sex of a child, either before conception or through selective abortion.

6 Description GPT5: Donating gametes; anonymity, parental claims, and compensation ethics.

6.1 Description Gemini: The study of ethical principles and moral or ethical problems that arise in a business environment. It covers issues from corporate governance to corporate social responsibility.
6.2 Description SEP: Exchange is fundamental to business. 'Business' can mean an activity of exchange. One entity (e.g., a person, a firm) "does business" with another when it exchanges a good or service for valuable consideration, i.e., a benefit such as money. 'Business' can also mean an entity that offers goods and services for exchange, i.e., that sells things. Target is a business. Business ethics can thus be understood as the study of the ethical dimensions of the exchange of goods and services, and of the entities that offer goods and services for exchange. This includes related activities such as the production, distribution, marketing, sale, and consumption of goods and services (cf. Donaldson & Walsh 2015; Marcoux 2006b). Questions in business ethics are important and relevant to everyone. Almost all of us "do business", or engage in a commercial transaction, almost every day. Many of us spend a major portion of our lives engaged in, or preparing to engage in, exchange activities, on our own or as part of organizations. Business activity shapes the world we live in, sometimes for good and sometimes for ill. Business ethics in its current incarnation is a relatively new field, growing out of research by moral philosophers in the 1970's and 1980's. But scholars have been thinking about the ethical dimensions of commerce at least since the Code of Hammurabi (c. 1750 BC). This entry summarizes research on central questions in business ethics, including: What sorts of things can be sold? How can they be sold? In whose interests should firms be managed? Who should manage them? What do firms owe their workers, and what do workers owe their firms? Should firms try to solve social problems? Is it permissible for them to try to influence political outcomes? Given the vastness of the field, of necessity certain questions are not addressed.

6.2 Description SEP: The concept of punishment-its definition-and its practical application and justification during the past half-century have shown a marked drift away from efforts to reform and rehabilitate offenders in favor of retribution and incarceration. Punishment in its very conception is now acknowledged to be an inherently retributive practice, whatever may be the further role of retribution as a (or the) justification or goal of punishment. A liberal justification of punishment would proceed by showing that society needs the threat and the practice of punishment, because the goal of social order cannot be achieved otherwise and because it is unfair to expect victims of criminal aggression to bear the cost of their victimization. Constraints on the use of threatened punishments (such as due process of law) are of course necessary, given the ways in which authority and power can be abused. Such a justification involves both deontological as well as consequentialist considerations.
