An Athenian citizen of high status, he displays in his works his absorption in the political events and intellectual movements of his time, but the questions he raises are so profound and the strategies he uses for tackling them so richly suggestive and provocative that educated readers of nearly every period have in some way been influenced by him, and in practically every age there have been philosophers who count themselves Platonists in some important respects. He was not the first thinker or writer to whom the word "philosopher" should be applied. But he was so self-conscious about how philosophy should be conceived, and what its scope and ambitions properly are, and he so transformed the intellectual currents with which he grappled, that the subject of philosophy, as it is often conceived-a rigorous and systematic examination of ethical, political, metaphysical, and epistemological issues, armed with a distinctive method-can be called his invention. Few other authors in the history of Western philosophy approximate him in depth and range: perhaps only Aristotle (who studied with him), Aquinas, and Kant would be generally agreed to be of the same rank.
6.3 Description SEP: Plato (427a 347 BCE) was the seminal Greek philosopher. Student of Socrates, teacher of Aristotle. He founded the Academy. His dialogues explore justice, virtue, definitions, and the Theory of Forms (abstract ideal realities). His work set the agenda for the entire Western tradition.

6 Description GPT5: Socrates: practitioner of the elenchus and ethical intellectualism, champion of the "examined life" and the primacy of virtue over wealth or power.
6.1 Description Gemini: The teacher of Plato, Socrates is famous for the Socratic method (elenchus), a form of inquiry based on asking and answering questions to stimulate critical thinking and expose contradictions. He famously claimed that "the unexamined life is not worth living."
6.2 Description SEP: Constantin Brancusi. Socrates Image The Museum of Modern Art; Licensed by Scala/Art Resource, NY 2005 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York/ADAGP, Paris reproduced with permission of the Brancusi Estate The philosopher Socrates remains, as he was in his lifetime (469-399 B.C.E.),[1] an enigma, an inscrutable individual who, despite having written nothing, is considered one of the handful of philosophers who forever changed how philosophy itself was to be conceived. All our information about him is second-hand and most of it vigorously disputed, but his trial and death at the hands of the Athenian democracy is nevertheless the founding myth of the academic discipline of philosophy, and his influence has been felt far beyond philosophy itself, and in every age. Because his life is widely considered paradigmatic for the philosophic life and, more generally, for how anyone ought to live, Socrates has been encumbered with the admiration and emulation normally reserved for founders of religious sects-Jesus or Buddha-strange for someone who tried so hard to make others do their own thinking, and for someone convicted and executed on the charge of irreverence toward the gods. Certainly he was impressive, so impressive that many others were moved to write about him, all of whom found him strange by the conventions of fifth-century Athens: in his appearance, personality, and behavior, as well as in his views and methods. So thorny is the difficulty of distinguishing the historical Socrates from the Socrateses of the authors of the texts in which he appears and, moreover, from the Socrateses of scores of later interpreters, that the whole contested issue is generally referred to as the Socratic problem. Each age, each intellectual turn, produces a Socrates of its own. It is no less true now that, "The 'real' Socrates we have not: what we have is a set of interpretations each of which represents a 'theoretically possible' Socrates," as Cornelia de Vogel (1955, 28) put it. In fact, de Vogel was writing as a new analytic paradigm for interpreting Socrates was about to become standard-Gregory Vlastos's model ( 2.2), which would hold sway until the mid 1990s. Who Socrates really was is fundamental to virtually any interpretation of the philosophical dialogues of Plato because Socrates is the dominant figure in most of Plato's dialogues.
6.3 Description SEP: Socrates (469a 399 BCE). The Archetypal Philosopher. Socratic Method (Elenchus): questioning to expose ignorance (aporia). 'The unexamined life is not worth living'. Intellectualism: Virtue is Knowledge; no one errs willingly. Executed by Athens.

6 Description GPT5: Schools after Alexander that treat philosophy as therapy-Stoicism, Epicureanism, Skepticism-aiming at ataraxia and rational agency.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period of philosophy from the death of Alexander the Great (323 BCE) to the rise of the Roman Empire. The focus of philosophy shifted from metaphysics to ethics, with the central goal being the achievement of personal tranquility (ataraxia).

6 Description GPT5: Stoics: cosmos ordered by logos and fate; only virtue is good; ethics of apathy (proper affect) and cosmopolitan duty.
6.1 Description Gemini: A school of Hellenistic philosophy founded by Zeno of Citium. The Stoics taught that virtue is the only good and that one should live in accordance with nature and reason (logos), remaining indifferent to external events beyond one's control.

6 Description GPT5: Epicureans: atomism, pleasure as aponia/ataraxia, valuation of friendship, and arguments to dispel fear of gods and death.
6.1 Description Gemini: A school of Hellenistic philosophy founded by Epicurus. It advocated for a form of hedonism, where the goal of life is pleasure, but defined pleasure as the absence of pain and mental disturbance (ataraxia).

6 Description GPT5: Neoplatonists: emanation from the One - Intellect - Soul, metaphysical hierarchy and mystical ascent (Plotinus).
6.1 Description Gemini: The final school of Greek philosophy, most associated with Plotinus. It reinterpreted Plato's philosophy, positing that all of reality emanates from a single, transcendent principle called "the One."

6 Description GPT5: Academic Skeptics: withhold assent via probabilism; challenge dogmatism while guiding action by the plausible.
6.1 Description Gemini: A school of Hellenistic skepticism, associated with Plato's Academy, that argued that certain knowledge is impossible and that one should doubt all claims.

6 Description GPT5: Pyrrhonists: achieve ataraxia through epoche (suspending judgment) by balancing equipollent arguments.
6.1 Description Gemini: A more radical school of skepticism founded by Pyrrho of Elis. Pyrrhonists advocated for the suspension of judgment (epoche) on all non-evident matters to achieve a state of mental tranquility.

6 Description GPT5: Pre-classical inquiries into cosmos and change, seeking arche (first principle) and naturalistic explanations for order.
6.1 Description Gemini: The earliest period of Greek philosophy, before Socrates. These thinkers were primarily concerned with cosmology and identifying the fundamental substance or principle (arche) of the universe.

6 Description GPT5: Milesians (Thales, Anaximander, Anaximenes): material arche (water/apeiron/air) and rational cosmology.
6.1 Description Gemini: The first group of Pre-Socratic philosophers from the city of Miletus, including Thales (who proposed water as the arche), Anaximander (apeiron, the boundless), and Anaximenes (air).

6 Description GPT5: Pythagoreans: number as the essence of things; harmony and mathematical structure in nature, plus transmigration of souls.
6.1 Description Gemini: A school of thought founded by Pythagoras, which held that the fundamental reality of the universe is mathematical and that all things can be understood through numerical relationships.

6 Description GPT5: Eleatics (Parmenides, Zeno): Being is one and unchanging; paradoxes against motion and plurality.
6.1 Description Gemini: A school of Pre-Socratic philosophy, including Parmenides and Zeno of Elea. They argued through logical deduction that reality is a single, unchanging, and indivisible whole, and that all change and motion are illusions.

6 Description GPT5: Pluralists (Empedocles, Anaxagoras): world from mixture of roots or seeds governed by Love/Strife or Nous.
6.1 Description Gemini: Pre-Socratic philosophers, such as Empedocles and Anaxagoras, who argued that reality is composed of multiple fundamental substances.

6 Description GPT5: Atomists (Leucippus, Democritus): reality as atoms and void; mechanistic causation and emergent qualities.
6.1 Description Gemini: Pre-Socratic philosophers, most notably Leucippus and Democritus, who proposed that the universe is composed of indivisible, indestructible particles called atoms moving in an infinite void.

6 Description GPT5: Sophists: itinerant rhetoricians teaching civic skill; provocations about relativism, nomos vs physis, and the power of logos.
6.1 Description Gemini: Itinerant teachers in ancient Greece who offered instruction in rhetoric and argumentation for a fee. Protagoras, a leading Sophist, famously claimed, "Man is the measure of all things."
6.2 Description SEP: The Greek word sophistes, formed from the noun sophia, 'wisdom' or 'learning', has the general sense 'one who exercises wisdom or learning'. As sophia could designate specific types of expertise as well as general sagacity in the conduct of life and the higher kinds of insight associated with seers and poets, the word originally meant 'sage' or 'expert'. In the course of the fifth century BCE the term, while retaining its original unspecific sense, came in addition to be applied specifically to a new type of intellectuals, professional educators who toured the Greek world offering instruction in a wide range of subjects, with particular emphasis on skill in public speaking and the successful conduct of life. The emergence of this new profession, which was an extension to new areas of the tradition of the itinerant rhapsode (reciter of poems, especially of Homer), was a response to various social, economic, political and cultural developments of the period. The increasing wealth and intellectual sophistication of Greek cities, especially Athens, created a demand for higher education beyond the traditional basic grounding in literacy, arithmetic, music and physical training. To some extent this involved the popularization of Ionian speculation about the physical world (see Presocratic Philosophy), which was extended into areas such as history, geography and the origins of civilization. The increase in participatory democracy, especially in Athens, led to a demand for success in political and forensic oratory, and hence to the development of specialized techniques of persuasion and argument. Finally, the period saw the flourishing of a challenging, rationalistic climate of thought on questions including those of morality, religion and political conduct, to which the sophists both responded and contributed. It is important to emphasize the individualistic character of the sophistic profession; its practitioners belonged to no organization, shared no common body of beliefs and founded no schools, either in the sense of academic institutions or in that of bodies of individuals committed to the promulgation of specific doctrines. In what follows we shall illustrate the diversity of sophistic activities, while considering the extent to which we can nevertheless identify common themes and attitudes.
6.3 Description SEP: Fifth-century BCE Greek intellectuals who taught rhetoric, philosophy, and practical skills for fees. Major sophists included Protagoras, Gorgias, and Prodicus. Though criticized by Plato and Aristotle, they contributed to epistemology, ethics, and political theory, with Protagoras's relativism and Gorgias's rhetoric particularly influential.

6 Description GPT5: Medieval synthesis of Greek philosophy with Abrahamic theology via scholastic method; Renaissance humanism revives philology and civic virtue.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period of philosophy between the fall of the Roman Empire and the beginning of the modern era. It is characterized by the attempt to synthesize Greek philosophy (particularly the works of Plato and Aristotle) with the doctrines of Christian theology.

6 Description GPT5: Early medieval (patristic to Carolingian): problems of divine attributes, illumination, universals, and classical transmission.
6.1 Description Gemini: The earliest period of medieval philosophy, dominated by the effort to reconcile Christian faith with classical learning.

6 Description GPT5: Augustine: Christian Platonism; illumination epistemology, analysis of time and will, and the two cities.
6.1 Description Gemini: A pivotal figure who integrated Platonic philosophy with Christian doctrine. His work addresses the problem of evil, divine illumination, original sin, and the nature of time.
6.3 Description SEP: Augustine of Hippo (354a 430) was the most influential Christian philosopher of antiquity. His works on sin, grace, free will, and the Trinity shaped Western theology and philosophy for over a millennium. Synthesizing Neoplatonism with Christian doctrine, he developed influential theories of time, memory, language, and knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: Boethius: conduit of Aristotle to the Latin West; logic and universals, and a theodicy of providence (Consolation of Philosophy).
6.1 Description Gemini: A Roman senator who served as a crucial bridge between the ancient and medieval periods. His work, The Consolation of Philosophy, was a highly influential text throughout the Middle Ages.
6.3 Description SEP: Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius (c. 475a 526) was a Roman philosopher who transmitted ancient philosophy to the Latin Middle Ages. He translated Aristotle's logical works, wrote commentaries and textbooks on logic, and authored theological treatises. His Consolation of Philosophy, written in prison, became one of the most influential philosophical works of the Middle Ages.

6 Description GPT5: Rise of schools and translation movement; debates on faith-reason, realism-nominalism, and logical method.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period that saw the rise of Scholasticism, a method of learning that emphasizes dialectical reasoning to resolve contradictions and extend knowledge.

6 Description GPT5: Abelard: conceptualism, dialectical sic et non, and an intention-based ethics.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French scholastic philosopher, theologian, and logician, famous for his affair with Heloise and his work on the problem of universals, where he developed a position known as conceptualism.
6.3 Description SEP: Peter Abelard (1079a 1142) was a preeminent medieval philosopher and theologian known for his contributions to logic, ethics, and metaphysics. His work significantly influenced scholastic thought, particularly through his dialectical method and analysis of universals.

6 Description GPT5: Anselm: ontological argument, "faith seeking understanding," and analysis of divine necessity.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Benedictine monk and philosopher best known for formulating the ontological argument for the existence of God, which argues that God's existence follows from the very concept of God.
6.3 Description SEP: Anselm of Canterbury (1033a 1109) was a medieval philosopher and theologian best known for creating the ontological argument for God's existence. His slogan 'faith seeking understanding' characterizes his rational approach to theology.

6 Description GPT5: Roger Bacon: advocacy of experiment and mathematics in natural philosophy; optics and empirical method.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher who was an early proponent of the scientific method and empirical inquiry.
6.2 Description SEP: Roger Bacon (1214/1220-1292), Master of Arts, contemporary of Robert Kilwardby, Peter of Spain, and Albert the Great at the University of Paris in the 1240s, was one of the early Masters who taught Aristotle's works on natural philosophy and metaphysics. Sometime after 1248-49, he became an independent scholar with an interest in languages, mastering the known Greek and Arabic texts on the science of optics. In 1256/57, either at Paris or Oxford, he joined the Franciscan Order. By 1262 he believed that his university reputation for advanced learning had suffered. And so, he sought the Patronage of Cardinal Guy le Gros de Foulque, Papal Ambassador to England (who later served as Pope Clement IV, 1265-68). On the instruction of the Pope on June 22, 1266, Bacon quickly wrote "an introductory work," the Opus maius, and the related works, Opus minus and Opus tertium. He set out his own new model for a reform of the system of philosophical, scientific, and theological studies, seeking to incorporate language studies and science studies, then unavailable, at the University of Paris. In this project, he was partly successful. He wrote a new and provocative text on semiotics, and influenced the addition of perspectiva to mathematical studies (the Quadrivium) as a required university subject. He succeeded in setting out a model of an experimental science on the basis of his study of optics. The latter was used in his extension of experimental science to include new medicines and the general health care of the body. He did this in a new context: the application of linguistic and scientific knowledge for a better understanding of Theology and in the service of the Res publica Christiana. Bonaventure and John Pecham were among his first readers. It is clear that with the possible exception of the uses or astrology and alchemy, Bacon shared in Bonaventure's project seeking a "reduction" of the sciences to theology. Bacon also found a sympathetic reader and interpreter in the great secular scholar of the Sorbonne, Peter of Limoges (d. 1306). He uses Bacon's optics for scientific and moral investigation. Bacon through the work of Peter of Limoges may have influenced Raymond Lull. Sometime in the late 1270s or early 1280s, Bacon returned to Oxford, where he completed his edition with introduction and notes of the Secretum secretorum, a Latin translation of an Arabic text on the education of the Prince, the Sirr-al-'asrar. He believed it to be a work by Aristotle written for Alexander the Great. This work of advice to the Prince points to Bacon's close connections to the Papal Court through Pope Clement IV, the French Court through Alphonse of Poitiers, and the English Court. His contacts with the Papal Curia were mediated by William Bonecor, Ambassador for King Henry III. Bacon died at Oxford c. 1292.
6.3 Description SEP: Roger Bacon (c. 1214a 1292) was a Franciscan philosopher and early advocate of empirical methods in science. He mastered Greek and Arabic scientific texts, particularly in optics, and emphasized experimental verification alongside rational argument. His work anticipated aspects of the later scientific revolution while remaining rooted in medieval scholasticism.

6 Description GPT5: High scholasticism; full reception of Aristotle, university disputation, and technical logic.
6.1 Description Gemini: The peak of the Scholastic period, which saw the founding of universities and the full recovery of Aristotle's works in the West.

6 Description GPT5: Aquinas: synthesis of Aristotle with Christian theology; Five Ways, hylomorphism, natural law.
6.1 Description Gemini: The most influential philosopher of the Scholastic period. Aquinas masterfully synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology in his Summa Theologica. His "Five Ways" are classic arguments for the existence of God.
6.3 Description SEP: Thomas Aquinas (c. 1225a 1274) was the greatest philosopher-theologian of the thirteenth century, epitomizing the scholastic method of medieval universities. Drawing extensively on Aristotle, he built a comprehensive synthesis of Christian theology and Aristotelian philosophy. His systematic works, especially the Summa Theologiae, remain central to Catholic thought and continue to influence philosophy today.

6 Description GPT5: Buridan: impetus theory in physics; sophismata and terminist logic bridging medieval and early modern science.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French philosopher and logician known for his work on the theory of impetus, a precursor to the modern concept of inertia.
6.3 Description SEP: John Buridan (c. 1300a 1361) was the most influential Parisian philosopher of the fourteenth century. He lectured on logic and Aristotle, producing commentaries on metaphysics, natural philosophy, and ethics. He is famous for developing nominalist logic, impetus theory in physics, and the paradox of 'Buridan's ass.'

6 Description GPT5: Ockham: nominalism and Ockham's razor; voluntarism, and distinction between intuitive and abstractive cognition.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English Franciscan friar and major figure in nominalism. He is most famous for the principle of parsimony known as Ockham's Razor ("entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity").
6.3 Description SEP: William of Ockham (c. 1287a 1347) was a Franciscan nominalist. He denied universals existed outside the mind. 'Ockham's Razor' (parsimony) advises against multiplying entities unnecessarily. He defended divine voluntarism and separation of church and state, influencing the Reformation and modern empiricism.

6 Description GPT5: Scotus: univocity of being, formal distinction, haecceity, and a robust voluntarism about will.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Scottish scholastic philosopher known for his complex metaphysical arguments, including his doctrine of univocity (that 'being' is predicated of God and creatures in the same sense) and haecceity (the "thisness" of an individual).

6 Description GPT5: Humanist revival of studia humanitatis and classical sources; emerging statecraft and textual criticism.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period of transition from the Middle Ages to modernity, marked by a revival of interest in classical art and literature (humanism) and a shift in philosophical focus toward political theory.

6 Description GPT5: Erasmus: Christian humanism, philology, and defense of free will against reforming determinisms.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Dutch humanist who was a leading scholar of the northern Renaissance. He advocated for church reform and emphasized a return to the original sources of Christianity.
6.3 Description SEP: Desiderius Erasmus (c. 1467a 1536) was a Renaissance humanist whose writings addressed nature versus nurture, language and meaning, ideal government, faith, and knowledge. Though not systematic, his Christian humanism, emphasis on education, and moderate reform influenced European thought. His debate with Luther addressed free will.

6 Description GPT5: Machiavelli: realist statecraft-virtu and fortuna, raison d'etat, and the autonomy of politics from morality.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Italian diplomat and political theorist whose book, The Prince, offers a pragmatic and often ruthless analysis of how to acquire and maintain political power. He is considered a founder of modern political science.
6.3 Description SEP: NiccolA2 Machiavelli (1469a 1527) wrote The Prince and Discourses, analyzing political power realistically. He argued effective rule may require immoral means and emphasized virtA1 (effective action) over virtue. Machiavelli's political realism influenced modern political thought, separating politics from ethics.

6 Description GPT5: Philosophy amid the Scientific Revolution: rationalism/empiricism, new mechanics, and social contract theories.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period from the early 17th to the late 18th century, also known as the Age of Reason and the Enlightenment. It was dominated by the great philosophical debate between Rationalism and Empiricism.

6 Description GPT5: British strand emphasizing experience in knowledge, political economy, and moral psychology.
6.1 Description Gemini: The tradition of philosophy, centered in Great Britain, that emphasized empiricism-the view that all knowledge originates in sensory experience.

6 Description GPT5: Bentham: utilitarian calculus-greatest happiness-and program of legal and institutional reform.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English utilitarian philosopher and social reformer who advocated for the principle of "the greatest happiness for the greatest number."
6.3 Description SEP: Jeremy Bentham (1748a 1832) was the founder of modern utilitarianism as a systematic moral and political theory. He developed the principle of utilitya that actions are right insofar as they promote the greatest happiness of the greatest number. His influence extended to legal reform, political philosophy, and the development of consequentialist ethics.

6 Description GPT5: Berkeley: immaterialism (esse est percipi); attack on abstract ideas and defense of common sense.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Irish philosopher and bishop who advanced a form of idealism known as immaterialism. He famously argued that things only exist to the extent that they are perceived (esse est percipi).
6.3 Description SEP: George Berkeley (1685a 1753), Bishop of Cloyne, was an early modern philosopher famous for defending idealisma the view that reality consists exclusively of minds and their ideas. He argued that material objects exist only as perceptions in minds, summarized in his principle 'esse est percipi' (to be is to be perceived). His major works include the Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge and Three Dialogues.

6 Description GPT5: Hobbes: materialism and mechanism; Leviathan and the sovereign via contract for peace.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher and political theorist whose work Leviathan argued that a strong, absolute sovereign is necessary to escape the brutal "state of nature." He was a key figure in social contract theory.
6.3 Description SEP: Thomas Hobbes (1588a 1679) developed materialist philosophy and social contract theory. His Leviathan argued natural state is war of all against all, justifying absolute sovereignty through social contract. Hobbes defended mechanistic metaphysics, nominalism, and psychological egoism. His political philosophy profoundly influenced modern political thought.

6 Description GPT5: Hume: radical empiricism and skepticism; habit theory of causation, problem of induction, sentimentalist ethics.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Scottish philosopher and a central figure of the Enlightenment. As a radical empiricist, he was deeply skeptical of claims about causation, induction, and the existence of a stable self.
6.3 Description SEP: David Hume (1711a 1776) developed empiricist philosophy challenging rationalism and religious belief. His Treatise analyzed causation, induction, personal identity, and passions. Hume argued reason is slave of passions, causation is constant conjunction, and inductive inference lacks rational foundation. His skepticism profoundly influenced subsequent philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Hutcheson: moral sense theory; benevolence and utility as sources of approval.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Scottish philosopher who was a key figure in the Scottish Enlightenment and a major influence on moral philosophy, known for his theory of the "moral sense."
6.3 Description SEP: Francis Hutcheson (1694a 1746) was a Scottish Enlightenment philosopher who developed moral sense theory. He argued that moral judgments derive from an internal moral sense producing approval or disapproval. Hutcheson influenced utilitarianism through his 'greatest happiness' formulations and shaped Hume's and Smith's moral philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Locke: theory of ideas, primary/secondary qualities, personal identity, property and limited government.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher who is considered the father of liberalism. He argued that the mind at birth is a tabula rasa (blank slate) and that all knowledge comes from experience. His political theory defended the natural rights of life, liberty, and property.
6.3 Description SEP: John Locke (1632a 1704) was a British empiricist whose Essay Concerning Human Understanding argued knowledge derives from experience. His political philosophy defended natural rights, consent, and limited government. Locke's Two Treatises influenced liberalism and American founding. He contributed to epistemology, metaphysics, and education.

6 Description GPT5: Newton: mathematical physics of gravitation and motion; models of scientific method and explanation.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English physicist and mathematician whose scientific revolution profoundly influenced the Enlightenment's confidence in reason and empirical observation.
6.3 Description SEP: Isaac Newton (1642a 1727) transformed science with Principia (laws of motion, universal gravity) and Opticks. Philosophically, he defended absolute space and time, inductive method, and divine design. Newton's mechanics and methodology defined the modern scientific worldview, influencing Kant and Hume.

6 Description GPT5: Reid: common-sense realism, direct perception, and critique of skeptical theory of ideas.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Scottish philosopher who was a founder of the "common sense" school of philosophy, which sought to rebut the skepticism of David Hume.
6.3 Description SEP: Thomas Reid (1710a 1796) was the founder of Scottish Common Sense Realism. He critiqued the 'Way of Ideas' (Locke/Hume) for leading to skepticism. Reid argued perception is direct and grounded in instinctive 'common sense' principles that are practically irresistible.

6 Description GPT5: Adam Smith: political economy (division of labor, invisible hand) and moral sentiments of sympathy.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Scottish economist and philosopher, author of The Wealth of Nations, who is considered the father of modern economics.

6 Description GPT5: French strand from Cartesian rationalism to Enlightenment moral-political theory.
6.1 Description Gemini: The tradition of philosophy in 17th and 18th century France, dominated by the figure of Descartes.

6 Description GPT5: Descartes: methodic doubt, cogito, dualism, and clear-and-distinct foundations for science.
6.1 Description Gemini: The French philosopher who is considered the father of modern philosophy. A leading rationalist, he sought to ground knowledge on a foundation of certainty, famously expressed in the phrase "Cogito, ergo sum" ("I think, therefore I am"). He also articulated a sharp distinction between mind and body (Cartesian dualism).
6.3 Description SEP: RenA Descartes (1596a 1650) is the father of modern philosophy, famous for methodological skepticism and 'cogito ergo sum.' He developed mind-body dualism, rationalist epistemology, and mechanistic physics. His Meditations revolutionized metaphysics, while his analytic geometry transformed mathematics.

6 Description GPT5: Malebranche: occasionalism and "vision in God," uniting Cartesianism with Augustinianism.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French rationalist philosopher who was a follower of Descartes, known for his theory of occasionalism.
6.3 Description SEP: Nicolas Malebranche (1638a 1715) synthesized Augustinianism and Cartesianism, developing occasionalism and Vision in God. God alone causes; creatures are occasions. We see ideas in God's mind. Malebranche's theodicy and continuous creation influenced modern philosophy despite Locke's and Leibniz's critiques.

6 Description GPT5: Pascal: critique of rationalism, wager for faith, and tensions between reason and heart.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French mathematician, physicist, and religious philosopher, known for "Pascal's Wager," an argument for the rationality of believing in God.
6.3 Description SEP: Blaise Pascal (1623a 1662) was a French mathematician, physicist, and Jansenist philosopher. His PensA es emphasize the misery and grandeur of the human condition (the 'thinking reed'). He critiqued rationalist theology, emphasizing 'reasons of the heart' and existential faith.

6 Description GPT5: Rousseau: general will, critique of inequality, and educational reform of the citizen (Emile).
6.1 Description Gemini: A French philosopher whose political philosophy, particularly his concept of the general will, influenced the French Revolution.
6.3 Description SEP: Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712a 1778). Enlightenment philosopher who critiqued civilization. 'Man is born free and everywhere he is in chains'. The Social Contract proposes the General Will as the source of legitimate sovereignty. Emile outlines education for natural freedom. Influenced the French Revolution and Romanticism.

6 Description GPT5: German strand from Leibnizian rationalism to Kantian critique and post-Kantian idealism.
6.1 Description Gemini: The tradition of philosophy in 17th and 18th century Germany, which included major rationalist thinkers and culminated in the work of Kant.

6 Description GPT5: Fichte: transcendental idealism-the I posits the not-I-emphasizing freedom and moral vocation.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German philosopher who was a founding figure of German Idealism, a movement that developed out of the work of Kant.

6 Description GPT5: Leibniz: monads, pre-established harmony, PSR (principle of sufficient reason), and optimism about the best world.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German rationalist philosopher and mathematician. He is known for the principle of sufficient reason and his optimistic view that our universe is the "best of all possible worlds."
6.3 Description SEP: G.W. Leibniz (1646a 1716) was a universal genius contributing to metaphysics, logic, mathematics, and physics. He developed monadology, pre-established harmony, and principle of sufficient reason. Leibniz invented calculus, defended optimism, and developed characteristic universalis. His rationalism profoundly influenced subsequent philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Kant: critical philosophy-synthetic a priori, categories, autonomy and the categorical imperative-recasting metaphysics and ethics.
6.1 Description Gemini: A monumental figure in modern philosophy who sought to synthesize the opposing schools of rationalism and empiricism. In his Critique of Pure Reason, he argued that knowledge is a product of both sensory experience and the mind's innate structures. His ethical theory is grounded in the Categorical Imperative.
6.3 Description SEP: Immanuel Kant (1724a 1804) is central modern philosopher who synthesized rationalism and empiricism in critical philosophy. His Critique of Pure Reason analyzed knowledge's conditions; Critique of Practical Reason developed deontological ethics via categorical imperative. Kant profoundly influenced epistemology, ethics, aesthetics, and political philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Nineteenth-century transformations: idealism, positivism, historicism, socialism, and early existential motifs.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period of philosophy following the work of Kant. It was marked by the rise of German Idealism, utilitarianism, pragmatism, and the precursors to existentialism.

6 Description GPT5: American developments-pragmatism and idealism-linking truth to inquiry, practice, and community.
6.1 Description Gemini: The school of philosophy that originated in the United States in the late 19th century, characterized by its focus on the practical consequences of ideas.

6 Description GPT5: James: pragmatism, will to believe, stream of consciousness, and truth as cash value in experience.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher and psychologist who was a leading figure in the pragmatist movement.
6.3 Description SEP: William James (1842a 1910) developed pragmatism and radical empiricism. His Principles of Psychology pioneered phenomenological psychology. James argued truth is what works, defended will to believe, and developed neutral monism. He profoundly influenced philosophy, psychology, and religious studies through anti-reductionist, pluralist approach.

6 Description GPT5: Peirce: pragmatic maxim, abduction, fallibilism, and triadic semiotics of sign-object-interpretant.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher, logician, and scientist who is considered the founder of pragmatism.
6.3 Description SEP: Charles Sanders Peirce (1839a 1914) was the founder of Pragmatism. A polymath in logic, semiotics, and science. His pragmatic maxim equates meaning with conceivable practical effects. He proposed a triadic sign theory, fallibilism, and an evolutionary cosmology (synechism, tychism).

6 Description GPT5: Royce: absolute idealism and the community of interpretation, ethics of loyalty.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American idealist philosopher who was a prominent figure in the late 19th century.
6.3 Description SEP: Josiah Royce (1855a 1916). American Absolute Idealist. Argued that errors imply an absolute truth (The Absolute) that embraces them. Developed a philosophy of 'Loyalty' to the Beloved Community. Engaged in fruitful debate with William James (Pragmatism vs. Absolute).

6 Description GPT5: Austrian currents joining empirical psychology and logic, precursors to analytic philosophy.
6.1 Description Gemini: The school of philosophy in 19th-century Austria, which emphasized detailed analysis of psychological phenomena and was a precursor to both analytic philosophy and phenomenology.

6 Description GPT5: Brentano: intentionality of mental acts; descriptive psychology as a basis for philosophy.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Austrian philosopher and psychologist whose work on intentionality (the "aboutness" of mental states) was a major influence on phenomenology.
6.3 Description SEP: Franz Brentano (1838a 1917) was an Austrian philosopher who reintroduced intentionality as the mark of the mental to modern philosophy. His empirical psychology, influenced by Aristotle and Scholasticism, classified mental phenomena by their mode of directedness toward objects. He profoundly influenced phenomenology, analytic philosophy, and Austrian philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Mach: phenomenalism and economy of thought; critique of atomism and metaphysics in science.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Austrian physicist and philosopher whose critical stance on metaphysics influenced the logical positivists.

6 Description GPT5: Meinong: theory of objects-including non-existent and subsistent-expanding intentional reference.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Austrian philosopher known for his complex "Theory of Objects," which distinguished between existent and non-existent objects.
6.3 Description SEP: Alexius Meinong (1853a 1920) developed the Theory of Objects (Gegenstandstheorie), categorizing everything that can be thought, including non-existent and impossible objects (like the round square). He distinguished between Sein (existence), Bestand (subsistence), and Aussersein (being-outside). Meinong's ontology influenced Russell (who critiqued it) and modern modal logic and fictionalism.

6 Description GPT5: British trajectories in utilitarianism and idealism, with debates on duty, value, and logic.
6.1 Description Gemini: The tradition of philosophy in 19th-century Britain, dominated by utilitarianism and idealism.

6 Description GPT5: Bradley: absolute idealism, doctrine of internal relations, and critique of relations in logic.
6.1 Description Gemini: A British idealist philosopher who was a leading figure in the late 19th century.
6.3 Description SEP: F.H. Bradley (1846a 1924) was the most influential British Idealist philosopher. His Appearance and Reality argued that ordinary experience is contradictory and that only the Absolute is fully real. He developed distinctive views on relations, truth, and the self that challenged both empiricism and common sense.

6 Description GPT5: Mill: refined utilitarianism, defense of liberty and women's rights, and a theory of induction.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher and economist who was a major proponent of utilitarianism. His work On Liberty is a classic defense of individual freedom and freedom of speech.
6.3 Description SEP: John Stuart Mill (1806a 1873) was a British empiricist and utilitarian. He developed the inductive methods of science (System of Logic) and defended liberty and individuality (On Liberty). Mill refined utilitarianism (qualitative pleasures) and supported women's rights (Subjection of Women), shaping liberal thought.

6 Description GPT5: Sidgwick: methods of ethics, intuitionism vs utilitarianism, and the dualism of practical reason.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English utilitarian philosopher who made significant contributions to moral and political philosophy.
6.3 Description SEP: Henry Sidgwick (1838a 1900). The Methods of Ethics. Analyzed three methods: Egoism, Intuitionism, and Utilitarianism. Exposed the 'Dualism of Practical Reason': logical tension between rational egoism and utilitarianism. Systematized classical utilitarianism.

6 Description GPT5: German innovations from idealism to materialism and the birth of modern logic.
6.1 Description Gemini: The dominant philosophical movement in 19th-century Germany, which developed from the work of Kant and included figures like Hegel, Fichte, and Schelling.

6 Description GPT5: Frege: modern predicate logic, sense/reference, and logicism in arithmetic.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German logician, mathematician, and philosopher who is considered one of the founders of analytic philosophy. His work revolutionized the field of logic.
6.3 Description SEP: Gottlob Frege (1848a 1925) revolutionized logic by developing the first predicate calculus with quantification theory. He attempted logicist reduction of arithmetic to logic in Grundgesetze but Russell's paradox revealed inconsistency. Frege's philosophy of language distinguished sense from reference, profoundly influencing analytic philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Hegel: absolute idealism, dialectic of Spirit, and historical phenomenology.
6.1 Description Gemini: A major figure in German Idealism who developed a comprehensive philosophical system based on a dialectical method of history, in which the Absolute Spirit unfolds through a process of thesis, antithesis, and synthesis.
6.3 Description SEP: G.W.F. Hegel (1770a 1831) developed dialectical idealism systematizing philosophy from logic to absolute spirit. His Phenomenology of Spirit traced consciousness's development; Science of Logic analyzed thought's categories. Hegel's philosophy emphasizes contradiction's productive role, historical development, and reason's self-realization, profoundly influencing subsequent philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Marx: historical materialism, critique of capitalism, alienation, and ideology.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German philosopher, economist, and revolutionary whose theories of historical materialism and communism, developed with Friedrich Engels, have had an immense global impact.
6.3 Description SEP: Karl Marx (1818a 1883) analyzed capitalism, history, and alienation using historical materialism. He argued economic structures determine social consciousness. Marx critiqued capitalism's exploitation and predicted its overthrow by the proletariat. His concepts (surplus value, class struggle, commodity fetishism) fundamentally shaped modern politics and social theory.

6 Description GPT5: Nietzsche: genealogy of morals, will to power, perspectivism, and critique of nihilism.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German philosopher who offered a radical critique of Christianity, morality, and Western philosophy. His key concepts include the will to power, the Ubermensch (Overman), the "death of God," and the eternal recurrence.
6.3 Description SEP: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844a 1900) was a German philosopher who critiqued traditional morality and Christianity ('God is dead'). He explored the Will to Power, the A bermensch, and Eternal Recurrence. Nietzsche diagnosed Western culture as nihilistic and sought a revaluation of all values to affirm life and creativity.

6 Description GPT5: Schelling: Naturphilosophie, identity of nature and mind, and a dynamic theory of freedom.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German idealist philosopher who was a contemporary of Hegel.
6.3 Description SEP: F.W.J. Schelling (1775a 1854). German Idealist. Naturphilosophie: Nature is visible Spirit; Spirit is invisible Nature. Identity Philosophy: Absolute is the indifference point of Subject and Object. Later philosophy focused on freedom, evil, and mythology.

6 Description GPT5: Schopenhauer: world as will and representation, pessimism, and ethics of compassion.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German philosopher known for his profound pessimism. He argued that the world is the manifestation of a blind, irrational, and ceaselessly striving metaphysical Will.
6.3 Description SEP: Arthur Schopenhauer (1788a 1860). Pessimist. The World as Will and Representation. The Kantian thing-in-itself is Will (blind striving). Life is suffering (oscillating between pain and boredom). Salvation via aesthetic contemplation (Art) or ascetic denial of the Will.

6 Description GPT5: Kierkegaard: existential stages, subjective truth, leap of faith, and critique of system.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Danish philosopher who is considered the first existentialist philosopher. He emphasized subjective truth, individual freedom, passion, and the necessity of a "leap of faith."
6.3 Description SEP: SA ren Kierkegaard (1813a 1855) pioneered existentialism, emphasizing individual existence, choice, and authentic faith. His pseudonymous works explored aesthetic, ethical, and religious life stages. Kierkegaard critiqued Hegelian system-building and Christendom's complacency, advocating passionate inwardness and subjective truth, profoundly influencing 20th-century philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Twentieth-century landscape marked by analytic/continental pluralism, the linguistic turn, phenomenology, and critical social theory.
6.1 Description Gemini: The period of philosophy marked by a fundamental split between the analytic and continental traditions.

6 Description GPT5: Anglophone movement prioritizing analysis of language, logic, and science, later expanding to mind, ethics, and politics.
6.1 Description Gemini: The dominant philosophical tradition in the English-speaking world. It is characterized by an emphasis on logical clarity, argumentation, and the analysis of language.

6 Description GPT5: Anscombe: intention and action, critique of consequentialism, revival of virtue ethics.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher who made significant contributions to ethics, particularly with her critique of modern moral philosophy and her role in reviving virtue ethics.
6.3 Description SEP: G.E.M. Anscombe (1919a 2001) was a British analytic philosopher and student of Wittgenstein. She made influential contributions to virtue ethics, philosophy of action (focusing on intention), and the philosophy of mind.

6 Description GPT5: Austin: ordinary-language philosophy and speech-act theory (locutionary/illocutionary/perlocutionary).
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher of language known for his theory of speech acts, which analyzes how language is used to perform actions.

6 Description GPT5: Ayer: logical positivism, verificationism, and emotivism in ethics.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher who was a leading figure in the logical positivist movement.
6.3 Description SEP: A.J. Ayer (1910a 1989) was the leading English representative of logical positivism. His Language, Truth and Logic (1936) championed verificationisma the view that meaningful statements must be empirically verifiable or analytically true. He continued the British empiricist tradition of Locke and Hume in twentieth-century philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Carnap: logical empiricism, explication, and linguistic frameworks for science.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German-American philosopher who was a member of the Vienna Circle and a leading figure in logical empiricism.
6.3 Description SEP: Rudolf Carnap (1891a 1970) was a leading logical positivist and one of the most important philosophers of the twentieth century. He developed the principle of verifiability, contributed to formal semantics and inductive logic, and championed the scientific philosophy project. His work spans philosophy of science, logic, language, and the foundations of mathematics.

6 Description GPT5: Chisholm: analytic epistemology (internalist justification), intentionality, and agent causation.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher known for his work in epistemology and metaphysics, particularly his response to the Gettier problem.
6.3 Description SEP: Roderick Chisholm (1916a 1999) was an influential American philosopher who worked across epistemology, metaphysics, ethics, and philosophy of mind. He defended foundationalism, developed theories of intentionality and agency, and constructed a systematic philosophy. His careful conceptual analysis and precise formulations shaped analytic philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: David Lewis: modal realism, counterpart theory, and decision-theoretic analyses of causation and value.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher known for his influential work in metaphysics, particularly his defense of modal realism (the view that possible worlds are real, concrete universes).
6.2 Description SEP: David Lewis (1941-2001) was one of the most important philosophers of the 20th Century. He made significant contributions to philosophy of language, philosophy of mathematics, philosophy of science, decision theory, epistemology, meta-ethics and aesthetics. In most of these fields he is essential reading; in many of them he is among the most important figures of recent decades. And this list leaves out his two most significant contributions. In philosophy of mind, Lewis developed and defended at length a new version of materialism (see the entry on physicalism). He started by showing how the motivations driving the identity theory of mind and functionalism could be reconciled in his theory of mind. He called this an identity theory, though his theory motivated the position now known as analytic functionalism. And he developed detailed accounts of mental content (building on Davidson's interpretationism) and phenomenal knowledge (building on Nemirow's ability hypothesis) that are consistent with his materialism. The synthesis Lewis ended up with is one of the central positions in contemporary debates in philosophy of mind. But his largest contributions were in metaphysics. One branch of his metaphysics was his Hume-inspired reductionism about the nomological. He developed a position he called "Humean supervenience", the theory that said that there was nothing to reality except the spatio-temporal distribution of local natural properties. And he did this by showing in detail how laws, chances, counterfactual dependence, causation, dispositions and colours could be located within this Humean mosaic. The other branch of his metaphysics was his modal realism. Lewis held that the best theory of modality posited concrete possible worlds. A proposition is possible if and only if it is true at one of these worlds. Lewis defended this view in his most significant book, On the Plurality of Worlds. Alongside this, Lewis developed a new account of how to think about modal properties of individuals, namely counterpart theory, and showed how this theory resolved several long-standing puzzles about modal properties.
6.3 Description SEP: David Lewis (1941a 2001) developed modal realism (possible worlds are concrete), counterfactual semantics, and functionalism about mind. His contributions span metaphysics, language, and philosophy of mind. Lewis defended Humean supervenience, counterpart theory, and best-system account of laws, profoundly influencing analytic philosophy.

6 Description GPT5: Davidson: anomalous monism, truth-theoretic semantics, and radical interpretation.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher who made significant contributions to the philosophies of language, mind, and action.
6.3 Description SEP: Donald Davidson (1917a 2003) was an influential American philosopher who developed truth-conditional semantics, anomalous monism about mind-body relations, and a coherence theory of knowledge. His work spans philosophy of language, mind, action, and epistemology. He defended holism and emphasized interpretation's role in understanding meaning.

6 Description GPT5: Goodman: new riddle of induction ("grue"), projectibility, and worldmaking.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher known for his work on induction (the "new riddle of induction") and his theory of symbols.
6.3 Description SEP: Nelson Goodman (1906a 1998) contributed to induction, nominalism, and aesthetics. His new riddle of induction (grue paradox) showed projectibility depends on entrenchment, challenging naA ve inductivism. Goodman developed nominalist philosophy rejecting abstract objects and analyzed art as symbol systems. His constructivalism emphasized worldmaking through concepts.

6 Description GPT5: Kuhn: paradigms, normal science, revolutions, and incommensurability in scientific change.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher and historian of science whose book, The Structure of Scientific Revolutions, introduced the concept of a "paradigm shift."

6 Description GPT5: Moore: common-sense philosophy, open-question argument, and Moorean shift against skepticism.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher who was a proponent of common sense and a critic of idealism. His work was foundational for the analytic tradition.
6.3 Description SEP: G.E. Moore (1873a 1958) was a founder of analytic philosophy. He defended common sense against idealism (Proof of an External World, 'Here is one hand'). Moore analyzed concepts and advocated analysis as philosophy's method, emphasizing clarity and ordinary language.

6 Description GPT5: Popper: falsificationism, conjectures and refutations, and critical rationalism.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Austrian-British philosopher of science who is known for his theory of falsifiability as the criterion for demarcating science from non-science.
6.3 Description SEP: Karl Popper (1902a 1994) was a philosopher of science and politics. He proposed Falsificationism: scientific theories cannot be proven, only falsified. In The Open Society and Its Enemies, he critiqued historicism (Plato, Hegel, Marx) and defended liberal democracy against totalitarianism.

6 Description GPT5: Quine: web of belief, indeterminacy of translation, ontological relativity, and naturalized epistemology.
6.1 Description Gemini: An influential American philosopher and logician who famously challenged the distinction between analytic and synthetic truths and the tenets of logical positivism.
6.3 Description SEP: W.V.O. Quine (1908a 2000). Giant of analytic philosophy. Destroyed the Analytic/Synthetic distinction ('Two Dogmas'). Advocated Holism (Duhem-Quine), Naturalized Epistemology, and Ontological Relativity (Indeterminacy of Translation). 'Philosophy of science is philosophy enough.'

6 Description GPT5: Rawls: justice as fairness, original position and veil of ignorance, political liberalism.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American political philosopher whose book, A Theory of Justice, revitalized political philosophy with its defense of a form of liberalism based on a social contract argument.
6.3 Description SEP: John Rawls (1921a 2002) revitalized political philosophy with A Theory of Justice (1971). Justice as Fairness: Veil of Ignorance leads to the Difference Principle (inequalities must benefit the worst off). Later, Political Liberalism addressed pluralism and stability.

6 Description GPT5: Rorty: neo-pragmatism, critique of representationalism, and contingency/solidarity.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher associated with pragmatism and postmodernism, who was a prominent critic of the traditional epistemological project of analytic philosophy.
6.3 Description SEP: Richard Rorty (1931a 2007) was a pragmatist and anti-representationalist. Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature attacked the idea that mind 'mirrors' reality. He advocated 'solidarity' over 'objectivity' and viewed philosophy as edifying conversation rather than a search for Truth.

6 Description GPT5: Russell: logical atomism, theory of descriptions, and foundations of analytic philosophy.
6.1 Description Gemini: A British philosopher, logician, and social critic who was one of the founders of analytic philosophy. His work Principia Mathematica, co-authored with Whitehead, was a landmark in the history of logic.
6.3 Description SEP: Bertrand Russell (1872a 1970). Founder of Analytic Philosophy. Logicism (math is logic). Theory of Descriptions ('The present King of France') solved referential puzzles. Logical Atomism. Defended Neutral Monism later in life. Public intellectual and pacifist.

6 Description GPT5: Ryle: critique of Cartesianism as category mistake; knowing-how vs knowing-that.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher who was a leading figure in the "ordinary language philosophy" movement. He is known for his critique of Cartesian dualism, which he famously ridiculed as the "ghost in the machine."
6.3 Description SEP: Gilbert Ryle (1900a 1976). Ordinary Language philosopher. The Concept of Mind (1949) attacked the 'Ghost in the Machine' (Cartesian Dualism) as a Category Mistake. Analyzed mental terms as dispositions to behave (Logical Behaviorism), not private inner events.

6 Description GPT5: Sellars: myth of the given, space of reasons, and scientific vs manifest images.
6.1 Description Gemini: An American philosopher known for his systematic and wide-ranging work, which sought to synthesize elements of American pragmatism and German idealism.
6.3 Description SEP: Wilfrid Sellars (1912a 1989). 'Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind'. Attacked the 'Myth of the Given' (idea of non-conceptual foundation for knowledge). Synoptic view: reconciling the Manifest Image (persons/reasons) with the Scientific Image (particles).

6 Description GPT5: Strawson: descriptive metaphysics, basic particulars, and reactive attitudes in morals.
6.1 Description Gemini: An English philosopher of language who was a key figure in the ordinary language philosophy movement.
6.3 Description SEP: P.F. Strawson (1919a 2006). Oxford Ordinary Language philosopher. 'On Referring' (critiqued Russell's Theory of Descriptions; sentences can lack truth value). Descriptive Metaphysics (Individuals): we identify particulars via a spatio-temporal framework. Freedom and Resentment (reactive attitudes).

6 Description GPT5: Tarski: semantic conception of truth (T-schema), object/metalanguage distinction.
6.1 Description Gemini: A Polish-American logician and mathematician who made major contributions to logic, model theory, and semantics.
6.3 Description SEP: Alfred Tarski (1901a 1983). Polish logician. Semantic Theory of Truth (Convention T). Model Theory (satisfaction). Logical Consequence defined as preservation of truth in all models. Banana Paradox (Banach-Tarski). One of the greatest 20th-century logicians.

6 Description GPT5: Wittgenstein: early picture theory (Tractatus); later language-games, forms of life, and rule-following.
6.1 Description Gemini: An Austrian-British philosopher who is one of the most important philosophers of the 20th century. His early work, the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus, was a major influence on logical positivism. His later work, the Philosophical Investigations, rejected many of his earlier views and introduced the concept of "language-games."
6.3 Description SEP: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889a 1951). Two phases. Early (Tractatus): Picture Theory of Meaning, logical atomism, 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent'. Late (Philosophical Investigations): Language Games, Meaning is Use, Private Language Argument, Family Resemblance.

6 Description GPT5: European movements-phenomenology, existentialism, hermeneutics, post-structuralism, and critical theory-focused on meaning, power, history, and subjectivity.
6.1 Description Gemini: A diverse set of philosophical traditions originating in mainland Europe. It includes phenomenology, existentialism, hermeneutics, structuralism, and post-structuralism.

6 Description GPT5: Adorno: negative dialectics, critique of the culture industry, and critical theory of society.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German philosopher and social theorist associated with the Frankfurt School of critical theory.
6.3 Description SEP: Theodor W. Adorno (1903a 1969) was a leading figure of the Frankfurt School of critical theory. His work integrates philosophy, sociology, and musicology to critique modern capitalist society and culture.

6 Description GPT5: Arendt: politics of action, natality, and analyses of totalitarianism and the public realm.
6.1 Description Gemini: A German-American political theorist whose work examined the nature of power, authority, and totalitarianism.
6.3 Description SEP: Hannah Arendt (1906a 1975) was a major twentieth-century political philosopher known for her analyses of totalitarianism, political action, and the human condition. A German-Jewish refugee who fled to the United States, she developed influential concepts including 'the banality of evil' and distinctions between labor, work, and action. Her thought emphasizes the importance of political participation and the fragility of freedom.

6 Description GPT5: Bergson: duration (duree), intuition vs intellect, and creative evolution.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French philosopher who emphasized the importance of intuition and duration in understanding reality.
6.3 Description SEP: Henri Bergson (1859a 1941) was an influential French philosopher known for his critiques of mechanism and emphasis on duration, intuition, and creative evolution. He argued that reality is fundamentally temporal and that intellectual analysis distorts lived experience. His work influenced phenomenology, process philosophy, and French philosophy broadly, experiencing renewed interest through Deleuze.

6 Description GPT5: de Beauvoir: feminist existentialism, ambiguity of freedom, and critique of patriarchy (The Second Sex).
6.1 Description Gemini: A French existentialist philosopher, feminist, and social theorist, most famous for her work The Second Sex, a foundational text of second-wave feminism.

6 Description GPT5: Deleuze: difference and repetition, immanence, and (with Guattari) rhizomatic social ontology.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French philosopher who was a key figure in post-structuralism, known for his concepts of difference and repetition.
6.3 Description SEP: Gilles Deleuze (1925a 1995) was a French philosopher who conceived philosophy as concept creation. His metaphysics replaces substance with multiplicity, being with becoming, and identity with difference. Difference and Repetition and A Thousand Plateaus (with Guattari) develop innovative ontologies addressing complexity, virtuality, and becoming.

6 Description GPT5: Derrida: deconstruction, differance, and critique of logocentrism and metaphysics of presence.
6.1 Description Gemini: A French philosopher who was the founder of deconstruction, a critical approach to analyzing the relationship between text and meaning.
6.3 Description SEP: Jacques Derrida (1930a 2004) founded deconstruction, a method of reading texts that reveals how binary oppositions and assumed hierarchies are unstable. His work challenges logocentrism and presence metaphysics. Influential in literary theory and continental philosophy, Derrida analyzed writing, diffA rance, and the impossibility of pure meaning.

6 Description GPT5: Foucault: power/knowledge, archaeology and genealogy, discipline and biopolitics.
